_meta.description: Schema-aligned exam questions for the New Grad Transition-to-Practice pathway

questions.stem: A nurse is assigned four patients at the start of a day shift. Patient A is a 68-year-old post-operative day one from a bowel resection, blood pressure 94/58 mmHg and heart rate 112 bpm on the last set of vitals from the previous shift. Patient B is a 45-year-old with stable COPD awaiting discharge. Patient C is a 72-year-old with controlled hypertension requesting a warm blanket. Patient D is a 55-year-old diabetic whose blood glucose at midnight was 6.4 mmol/L and who is now requesting breakfast. Which patient should the nurse assess first?
choices: A. Patient A, the post-operative patient with haemodynamic instability
B. Patient B, because COPD can deteriorate rapidly
C. Patient C, because comfort needs should be addressed promptly
D. Patient D, to verify the glucose before breakfast
questions.rationale: Patient A meets ABCDE 'C' criteria (Circulation) for an immediate threat: a systolic blood pressure of 94 mmHg in a post-operative patient who was likely normotensive pre-surgery represents significant haemodynamic compromise. Post-operative day one is the highest-risk window for concealed bleeding, third-spacing, and haemodynamic decompensation. This patient requires immediate bedside assessment, IV fluid assessment, and physician notification. The tachycardia is compensatory and together with the hypotension indicates an ongoing clinical emergency.

incorrectRationales.B: Patient B has a known, stable chronic condition and is awaiting discharge — they are not in acute physiological jeopardy. COPD can deteriorate, but there is no indication from the scenario that this patient is currently unstable.
incorrectRationales.C: Comfort requests, while important, do not represent a clinical urgency. A request for a warm blanket is a Tier 3 (can wait) demand that should be deferred until after the acute patient is assessed.
incorrectRationales.D: Although a blood glucose of 6.4 mmol/L should be monitored, it is within acceptable range and the patient is stable. Verifying glucose before breakfast is a Tier 2 task that can wait until the haemodynamically compromised patient is seen.

questions.stem: A nurse is applying Maslow's hierarchy to prioritize care for five patients. Which patient represents the highest physiological priority requiring immediate nursing action?
choices: A. A patient requesting reassurance about upcoming surgery scheduled for tomorrow
B. A patient reporting a pain score of 8/10 following their morning physical therapy session
C. A patient whose respiratory rate has increased from 16 to 26 breaths per minute over the past hour and who now requires 4L/min supplemental oxygen
D. A patient who has not eaten breakfast and is requesting help choosing a meal from the menu
questions.rationale: Under Maslow's hierarchy, oxygenation is a Level 1 physiological survival need and the highest clinical priority. A respiratory rate of 26 with increasing oxygen requirements represents an active breathing threat (the 'B' in ABCDE) that, if unaddressed, can rapidly progress to respiratory failure. This patient requires immediate assessment, consideration of rapid response activation, and physician notification. Breathing threats always outrank pain management and psychosocial needs.

incorrectRationales.A: Anxiety about upcoming surgery is a Level 3–4 psychosocial need (belonging, esteem). While important, it does not represent a physiological threat and must be deferred when a patient has an active breathing emergency.
incorrectRationales.B: Pain rated 8/10 post-physical therapy is a Level 2 need (safety and comfort) and warrants attention — but it is not immediately life-threatening. Pain management can be addressed after the respiratory emergency is stabilized.
incorrectRationales.D: Hunger is a Level 1 need, but choosing a meal from a menu indicates the patient is stable and cognitively intact. The meal is not the immediate physiological threat that a respiratory rate of 26 with increasing O2 requirements represents.

questions.stem: A nurse receives a critical lab result: a serum potassium of 2.4 mEq/L for a patient currently receiving IV furosemide for heart failure. The patient is alert and on telemetry. Which sequence of nursing actions is correct?
choices: A. Notify the physician, then go to the bedside to assess the patient
B. Go to the bedside to assess the patient, then notify the physician using SBAR, then document the notification
C. Document the critical value in the chart, then notify the physician, then assess the patient
D. Hold the furosemide, reassess potassium in two hours, then notify the physician if the level worsens
questions.rationale: The correct sequence for a critical lab value is: assess the patient at the bedside first to gather clinical data (cardiac rhythm, neuromuscular status, current symptoms), then call the physician with a complete SBAR that includes your firsthand assessment findings, then document the critical value notification, time, response, and actions taken. Assessing before calling ensures that the physician receives meaningful clinical information rather than just a lab number. Hypokalaemia at 2.4 mEq/L places this patient at high risk for life-threatening arrhythmia, particularly given active diuresis.

incorrectRationales.A: Calling the physician before bedside assessment means communicating a critical value without firsthand clinical findings. The physician will immediately ask about the cardiac rhythm, current symptoms, and vital signs — having those answers ready makes the call clinically complete and leads to better management decisions.
incorrectRationales.C: Documentation should follow assessment and notification, not precede them. Documenting a critical value before acting delays the patient's care and creates a legal record in the wrong sequence.
incorrectRationales.D: A potassium of 2.4 mEq/L in a patient on active diuretic therapy is a critical value requiring immediate physician notification — it cannot be safely managed by monitoring alone. Delaying notification when the patient is on telemetry and at arrhythmia risk is a patient safety error.

questions.stem: A nurse has five patients with the following medication orders due at 09:00. The nurse can only begin administering medications at 09:05 due to a brief urgent intervention for another patient. Which medication should the nurse administer first?
choices: A. Amlodipine 5 mg oral for a patient with hypertension, blood pressure 138/86 mmHg
B. Metformin 500 mg oral for a patient with well-controlled type 2 diabetes
C. Piperacillin-tazobactam IV for a patient admitted 90 minutes ago with a diagnosis of sepsis
D. Atorvastatin 40 mg oral for a patient with stable coronary artery disease
questions.rationale: The first antibiotic dose in sepsis is a critical time-sensitive intervention. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommend initiating antibiotics within one hour of sepsis recognition. With the patient admitted 90 minutes ago and antibiotics still not given, any further delay directly increases the risk of septic shock and mortality. The furosemide, metformin, and atorvastatin are all important medications for stable chronic conditions but have no comparable mortality risk associated with a short delay.

incorrectRationales.A: Amlodipine for hypertension with a well-controlled blood pressure is a chronic maintenance medication. A brief delay of 15 to 30 minutes for a stable patient with a BP of 138/86 does not create a clinical risk comparable to delayed sepsis antibiotics.
incorrectRationales.B: Metformin for well-controlled type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic medication. Missing a single dose by a short interval does not create acute patient risk, unlike a first antibiotic dose in sepsis.
incorrectRationales.D: Atorvastatin is a lipid-lowering agent for chronic cardiovascular risk reduction. A brief delay in this medication carries no acute safety risk and should be deferred when a sepsis patient requires urgent antibiotic administration.

questions.stem: Select all nursing actions that are appropriate when a nurse notices that a patient who was alert and oriented at 08:00 is now drowsy and responding only to verbal stimuli at 10:30, without an obvious documented cause.
choices: A. Perform a bedside blood glucose check immediately
B. Check the patient's SpO2 and respiratory rate
C. Document 'patient drowsy, will continue to monitor hourly' and reassess at 11:30
D. Notify the charge nurse and physician using SBAR
E. Review recent medications for sedating agents administered in the past two to four hours
F. Reassure the patient's family that drowsiness is normal after hospitalization
questions.rationale: A drop in level of consciousness from alert and oriented to responding only to verbal stimuli represents a 3-point decrease on the AVPU scale and constitutes an acute neurological change that requires immediate systematic assessment. The correct actions are: (A) check bedside glucose, as hypoglycaemia is immediately reversible and common; (B) assess SpO2 and respiratory rate, as hypoxia is a leading cause of sudden LOC decline; (D) notify the charge nurse and physician with a complete SBAR, as this is an acute change requiring medical evaluation; (E) review recent medications, as opioids, benzodiazepines, and sedating antihistamines are common iatrogenic causes of LOC changes. Every correct action is part of a systematic response to an acute neurological finding.

incorrectRationales.C: Documenting 'will continue to monitor' and waiting one hour is an inadequate response to an acute change in level of consciousness. A 3-step drop in AVPU (Alert to Voice) is a NEWS2 score trigger for urgent clinical review and cannot safely be deferred for an hour.
incorrectRationales.F: Reassuring the family without a clinical explanation or completed assessment is both inaccurate and professionally inappropriate. Drowsiness is not a normal consequence of hospitalization — it is a clinical finding that requires investigation before any family communication.

questions.stem: A nurse on a medical-surgical floor has five patients. At 10:15, the following events occur within a two-minute window: the patient in Room 302 activates the call bell requesting a pain medication refill; the patient in Room 305, a post-operative day one from abdominal surgery, is found by a PSW to have a saturated abdominal dressing; the patient in Room 308 wants to speak with the nurse about discharge instructions; and the patient in Room 310 is requesting help adjusting their television. In what order should the nurse respond?
choices: A. Room 305, Room 302, Room 308, Room 310
B. Room 302, Room 305, Room 310, Room 308
C. Room 308, Room 305, Room 302, Room 310
D. Room 310, Room 302, Room 308, Room 305
questions.rationale: Room 305 (post-op day one with saturated abdominal dressing) is a Tier 1 priority because a saturated surgical dressing indicates active or ongoing blood loss, which represents a potential 'C' (Circulation) threat on the ABCDE framework. A post-operative patient with a saturated wound requires immediate RN assessment to determine the nature and rate of bleeding, vital signs, and potential need for physician notification. Room 302 (pain medication request) is Tier 2 — pain management is important but not immediately life-threatening. Room 308 (discharge instructions) is Tier 3 — scheduled patient education. Room 310 (TV adjustment) is Tier 3 — a comfort request that poses no safety risk.

incorrectRationales.B: Addressing pain before a post-operative patient with a saturated wound reverses the clinical priority. Pain is important but does not carry the same immediate safety risk as potential haemorrhage in a post-operative patient.
incorrectRationales.C: Discharge instructions are a scheduled, non-urgent activity. Prioritizing this over either pain management or a potentially bleeding post-operative patient is clinically unsafe.
incorrectRationales.D: Adjusting a television is a comfort task with no clinical urgency. Attending to this before any of the other patients — particularly the one with the saturated dressing — demonstrates no clinical prioritization framework.

questions.stem: A patient who takes phenytoin 200 mg orally twice daily for seizure disorder is now NPO in preparation for a colonoscopy scheduled in four hours. The nurse notes the morning dose has not been administered. Which action is most appropriate?
choices: A. Hold the phenytoin until the patient is NPO-cleared post-procedure, as missing one dose is not clinically significant
B. Contact the physician to request an IV formulation or alternative route for the scheduled dose
C. Administer the oral phenytoin with a small sip of water, as anti-epileptics are exempt from NPO requirements
D. Document the dose as held and place a note for the post-procedure nurse to double the next dose
questions.rationale: Anti-epileptic medications are classified as time-critical because missing a dose can precipitate breakthrough seizures, which carry risk of injury, aspiration, and status epilepticus. NPO status does not automatically exempt essential medications from being administered — the nurse's correct action is to contact the physician to request an IV equivalent or alternative route to ensure the dose is not missed. Many institutions have NPO medication exception policies specifically for anti-epileptics, cardiovascular medications, and certain psychiatric drugs.

incorrectRationales.A: While a single missed dose may not always trigger a seizure, it significantly increases seizure risk — particularly in patients who are already at or near the therapeutic threshold. Anti-epileptics are among the drug classes for which missed doses carry the greatest consequence. 'Not clinically significant' is incorrect.
incorrectRationales.C: Anti-epileptics are not categorically exempt from NPO requirements. Administering an oral medication with water to an NPO patient without physician authorization violates both the NPO order and professional medication administration standards.
incorrectRationales.D: Doubling the subsequent dose of phenytoin is dangerous and not clinically indicated. Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index and non-linear pharmacokinetics — dose doubling increases toxicity risk without providing superior seizure control.

questions.stem: A nurse is about to delegate vital sign collection to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). Which of the following patients would be appropriate for the UAP to collect routine vital signs without requiring the nurse to be immediately present?
choices: A. A 70-year-old patient admitted two hours ago with a chief complaint of sudden onset chest pain, now pain-free but awaiting cardiac workup
B. A 55-year-old patient with well-controlled hypertension, admitted for an elective knee replacement on post-operative day three, ambulating independently, and expected to be discharged tomorrow
C. A 63-year-old patient with sepsis whose blood pressure was 88/54 mmHg on the previous hourly check
D. A 78-year-old patient whose level of consciousness has decreased since the morning shift
questions.rationale: The fifth right of delegation — Right Circumstances — requires that the patient be in a stable, predictable clinical state for UAP involvement to be appropriate. The 55-year-old on post-operative day three from a knee replacement, who is stable, independent, and near discharge, fits this criterion. Their care is predictable, their condition is stable, and vital sign collection is a routine task with no acute clinical complexity. All other patients represent either unknown acuity (chest pain workup), active physiological instability (sepsis with hypotension), or an acute neurological change — all of which require RN-level assessment.

incorrectRationales.A: A patient admitted for acute chest pain who is pain-free but awaiting a cardiac workup has not yet been cleared of acute coronary syndrome or other serious pathology. This patient's acuity is unknown and potentially high — vital signs require RN assessment and interpretation, not UAP collection.
incorrectRationales.C: A blood pressure of 88/54 mmHg in a sepsis patient represents active haemodynamic instability. Vital signs in this patient should be collected and immediately interpreted by the RN — delegation to UAP in this context is unsafe and constitutes a five-rights-of-delegation violation (Right Circumstances).
incorrectRationales.D: An acute decrease in level of consciousness is a neurological emergency. This patient requires an immediate RN assessment — not scheduled vital sign collection by UAP. The change in LOC itself is the clinical finding that must be assessed and escalated.

questions.stem: Select all tasks that a registered nurse (RN) can safely and appropriately delegate to a registered practical nurse (RPN) or licensed practical nurse (LPN) on a general medical-surgical floor.
choices: A. Performing a complete head-to-toe admission assessment for a newly admitted patient
B. Administering scheduled oral medications to a stable patient with type 2 diabetes admitted for a urinary tract infection
C. Developing a nursing care plan for a patient with a new diagnosis of heart failure
D. Reinforcing previously taught wound care instructions for a patient who is being discharged tomorrow
E. Performing a dressing change on a stable, healing surgical wound on post-operative day five
F. Deciding to hold a scheduled antihypertensive medication based on the patient's blood pressure reading
questions.rationale: RPN/LPN scope includes administration of scheduled oral medications to stable patients (B), reinforcement of previously established patient teaching (D — not initiating new teaching or creating the plan, but reinforcing what has already been taught), and routine wound care on predictable, stable wounds (E). These tasks involve predictable outcomes for stable patients and are within the regulated scope of RPNs and LPNs in most Canadian provinces and US states.

incorrectRationales.A: Initial comprehensive admission assessments require RN-level professional judgment. The admission assessment is the foundation of the nursing care plan and requires the independent clinical assessment and interpretation that defines RN practice.
incorrectRationales.C: Care plan development is a core nursing process function that requires RN-level assessment, nursing diagnosis, and goal-setting. RPNs and LPNs may contribute to care plan implementation but the development of the plan is an RN accountability.
incorrectRationales.F: Deciding to hold a medication based on a clinical parameter (blood pressure reading) requires nursing judgment and assessment — this is an RN function. While an RPN may administer the medication, the decision to hold it and the clinical reasoning behind it remain within RN scope.

questions.stem: A nurse delegates routine vital sign collection to a PSW for a stable patient in Room 406. Twenty minutes later, the PSW reports: 'The patient in 406 seems really confused today — she kept calling me by her daughter's name.' What is the nurse's most appropriate immediate response?
choices: A. Thank the PSW and ask them to document the observation in the nursing notes
B. Go to the bedside immediately to perform a neurological assessment
C. Ask the PSW to recheck the vital signs and report back if the patient becomes more confused
D. Call the patient's family to ask whether the confusion is typical for her
questions.rationale: When a delegatee reports a clinical observation — particularly an acute change in cognition — the RN is required to assess the patient directly and immediately. The PSW's report of confusion (misidentifying the PSW as her daughter) is a clinical finding, not simply a behavioral curiosity. Acute confusion can indicate hypoglycaemia, hypoxia, hyponatraemia, sepsis, stroke, medication toxicity, or many other serious clinical conditions. The RN must perform a firsthand neurological assessment and compare to the patient's documented baseline before any other action.

incorrectRationales.A: PSWs do not independently document clinical assessment findings in nursing notes — that is an RN function. More critically, asking the PSW to document rather than going to the bedside delays the clinical assessment that this finding requires.
incorrectRationales.C: Asking the PSW to recheck vital signs without the RN going to the bedside is an inappropriate delegation response. Vital sign values alone will not reveal the cause of the confusion, and this approach delays the RN assessment that is the professional standard.
incorrectRationales.D: Calling the family to ask about the patient's baseline is not the first action — assessing the patient is. Family communication may be appropriate after the assessment is complete and the clinical picture is clearer.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse is managing a four-patient assignment and is preparing insulin for the patient in Room 412. A colleague approaches and says, 'Can you help me turn Mrs. Park in 408 for two minutes — I can't do it alone?' Which response best reflects safe medication preparation practice?
choices: A. Leave the drawn-up insulin on the medication cart and help the colleague, then return to administer it
B. Stop the insulin preparation at the current step, place the materials in a designated hold position, help the colleague, then return and restart the preparation from the last verified step
C. Administer the insulin quickly before helping the colleague to avoid missing the scheduled time
D. Delegate insulin preparation to the RPN colleague while helping with the patient turn
questions.rationale: Interruptions during medication preparation are a primary cause of medication errors, particularly for high-alert medications like insulin. The correct approach is to pause at a defined stopping point, place materials in a designated 'hold' position (not back in the drawer), assist with the immediate need, then return and restart from the last verified step — not continue from an unremembered point. This process — sometimes called the Read-Back-and-Pause technique — ensures that no verification step is skipped during the re-entry. Simply returning and continuing from where you think you were dramatically increases the risk of error.

incorrectRationales.A: A drawn-up syringe of insulin left unattended on a medication cart creates multiple safety risks: the syringe could be confused with another medication, the patient could be harmed if someone else administers it without the five-right check, and the nurse returns to the preparation without knowing which steps were or were not completed.
incorrectRationales.C: Rushing through insulin administration to free up time for another task is a known error pattern. Insulin is a high-alert medication — rapid administration without complete verification creates significant patient harm risk. Speed is never a justification for skipping high-alert medication safety steps.
incorrectRationales.D: Insulin preparation and administration should not be delegated mid-process to another nurse without a complete restart and verification by that nurse. Transferring a partially prepared high-alert medication between providers without a complete re-verification is a safety violation.

questions.stem: A nurse receives report at 07:00 and plans to assess their five patients systematically. At 07:45, a rapid response is called in an adjacent room. The nurse responds to help and is occupied for 35 minutes. Which action should the nurse take first after the rapid response is resolved?
choices: A. Begin end-of-shift documentation to avoid a backlog later in the day
B. Notify the charge nurse about which tasks were deferred and request support for those patients
C. Return to the rapid response room to complete the documentation for that patient
D. Call all five patient families to explain the delay
questions.rationale: After an unexpected event derails the shift plan, the nurse's first action is to communicate with the charge nurse about which tasks were deferred and request coverage for patients who have not yet been assessed. This is the Stop–Sort–Sequence model applied to mid-shift replanning: stop, assess what was deferred, notify the charge nurse, and redistribute with team support. Three to four patients who have not yet received an initial assessment represent a patient safety gap that requires immediate team communication, not individual management in isolation.

incorrectRationales.A: Beginning documentation during a time when patients have not been assessed prioritizes administrative tasks over clinical care. Documentation is important, but it is not the priority in the immediate aftermath of a 35-minute gap in patient care.
incorrectRationales.C: Documentation for the rapid response patient is important but should not delay the nurse's return to their own unassessed patients. The rapid response team and that patient's primary nurse can manage the documentation; your unassessed patients need attention.
incorrectRationales.D: Calling five patient families to explain a delay is not a first action — it is not even a necessary action unless a specific clinical event needs to be communicated. This response prioritizes perception management over patient safety.

questions.stem: A nurse is about to leave at the end of their shift when they realize they did not document the result of a critical potassium of 2.8 mEq/L that was reported to them at 14:30. The result was addressed at the time — the physician was notified and IV replacement was ordered and administered — but no nursing note was entered. It is now 19:30. Which action is most appropriate?
choices: A. Do not enter any note, as the medication administration record will show the potassium replacement was given
B. Enter a late entry clearly labeled with the current date and time and the time of the clinical event, documenting the critical value, physician notification, and nursing actions taken
C. Ask the oncoming nurse to enter a note on your behalf, as you are no longer responsible after your shift has ended
D. Enter the note with the timestamp of 14:30 to ensure it appears in correct chronological order in the chart
questions.rationale: A late entry is a legitimate and professionally expected form of documentation when a clinical event occurred but was not charted at the time. The correct process is to enter the note clearly labeled as a late entry, documenting both the current date and time of the entry and the time of the actual clinical event: 'Late entry — documenting events of [date] at 14:30. Critical potassium value of 2.8 mEq/L received from laboratory. Patient assessed at bedside, physician notified by telephone using SBAR at 14:45. IV potassium replacement ordered and administered as per chart.' This accurately represents what occurred without misrepresenting the timeline.

incorrectRationales.A: The medication administration record documents that a medication was given — it does not document the critical value receipt, the physician notification, the content of the SBAR call, or the physician's response. These elements require a nursing note and are legally required elements of the care record.
incorrectRationales.C: Asking another nurse to document an event they did not participate in or witness is a documentation falsification. Every nurse documents their own clinical actions and assessments — the responsibility for this documentation does not transfer to the oncoming nurse.
incorrectRationales.D: Entering a note with a timestamp that does not reflect when the note was actually entered is falsification of the medical record, regardless of intent. The entry must be timestamped with the actual time it is written, with the clinical event time noted within the note body.

questions.stem: A nurse is reviewing the nursing assessment note entered by the previous nurse, which states: 'Patient doing well. Lungs clear. No complaints. Resting comfortably.' The current nurse is about to enter their own assessment of the same patient. Which statement accurately describes the documentation standard for this situation?
choices: A. The existing note is complete; the nurse can chart 'assessment findings unchanged from previous entry' to save time
B. The nurse must document their own objective assessment findings from their current evaluation, regardless of what the previous nurse documented
C. The nurse can ask the previous nurse to add more detail to their existing entry if the note is incomplete
D. The brief assessment note is acceptable because it documents that no complaints were reported
questions.rationale: Every nursing assessment must be independently documented with specific, objective findings reflecting the actual assessment performed by the current nurse at the current time. The previous note is vague, non-specific, and lacks the clinical detail required for a legally defensible nursing assessment. Charting 'unchanged' without specific findings constitutes a copied-forward assessment — a known documentation error associated with missed clinical changes. A nurse's assessment documentation must reflect what they personally found when they assessed the patient.

incorrectRationales.A: Charting 'unchanged from previous entry' without performing and documenting your own assessment is a documentation error. 'Unchanged' is only appropriate if it refers to specific findings that were previously documented and are genuinely still present — not as a substitute for independent assessment documentation.
incorrectRationales.C: Asking the previous nurse to amend their documentation does not address the current nurse's own assessment obligation. Each nurse is responsible for documenting their own clinical findings — the previous nurse's note is a separate legal record.
incorrectRationales.D: 'Patient doing well, lungs clear, no complaints, resting comfortably' is not a clinically specific assessment. It provides no objective data, no system-specific findings, and no comparison to baseline. Accreditation standards and professional practice standards require specific, objective nursing assessment documentation.

questions.stem: A nurse receives a telephone order from a physician for 'metformin 1000 mg, orally, twice daily.' The nurse knows the patient's most recent serum creatinine is 2.4 mg/dL. Which action should the nurse take?
choices: A. Accept the order as given, perform a read-back, transcribe it, and administer the medication as ordered
B. Decline the order, as this dose is above the recommended maximum for any patient
C. Question the order by clearly communicating the patient's creatinine level and requesting clarification before accepting or transcribing it
D. Accept the order and document a nursing concern in the chart, then administer the medication as ordered
questions.rationale: Metformin is contraindicated or requires dose adjustment in patients with significant renal impairment. A serum creatinine of 2.4 mg/dL suggests moderate to severe renal dysfunction (GFR likely below 30 mL/min/1.73m²), which is a contraindication to metformin use due to the risk of lactic acidosis — a potentially fatal metabolic complication. The nurse's professional obligation is to communicate the clinical concern clearly to the physician before accepting or transcribing the order: 'I'm noting the patient's most recent creatinine is 2.4 — I want to clarify whether metformin is appropriate given this level of renal function.' This is a respectful, clinical, and professionally required question.

incorrectRationales.A: Accepting and administering a medication order that appears to conflict with the patient's clinical parameters without questioning it is a medication safety error. The nurse is the final safety check before a drug reaches the patient — this responsibility requires clinical judgment, not passive acceptance.
incorrectRationales.B: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily is within the typical adult dosing range for patients with normal renal function — the issue is not the dose itself but the appropriateness of the medication given this patient's renal function. Declining based on dose alone misidentifies the actual clinical concern.
incorrectRationales.D: Documenting a concern and then administering the medication anyway does not constitute safe practice. Documenting a concern while proceeding with administration demonstrates awareness of the risk without preventing it — this is professionally and legally indefensible.

questions.stem: A nurse receives a telephone order for hydromorphone 2 mg IV push every three hours as needed for pain. What is the correct sequence before implementing this order?
choices: A. Write down the order, read it back completely, receive physician confirmation, transcribe in the MAR, then administer
B. Administer the medication immediately to relieve patient pain, then document the verbal order
C. Transcribe the order in the MAR, then call the pharmacy to verify the dose, then administer
D. Repeat the order back as you understood it, ask for written confirmation before administering
questions.rationale: The complete sequence for receiving a telephone order is: (1) write the order down as the physician dictates it, (2) read it back verbatim and completely, (3) receive explicit verbal confirmation from the physician, (4) transcribe the verified order into the MAR or EHR, then (5) administer. This sequence is mandated by both The Joint Commission (US) and Accreditation Canada as a patient safety standard. Hydromorphone is a high-alert opioid — the read-back is especially critical because sound-alike dosing errors (e.g., 2 mg vs 0.2 mg) can be fatal.

incorrectRationales.B: Administering a medication before documenting a verbal order means the medication is given without a legally verified order in the record. This places both the patient and the nurse at risk. Documentation of the order must precede administration.
incorrectRationales.C: Calling the pharmacy to verify a dose before transcribing reverses the safety sequence. The nurse should first verify the order through read-back with the physician, then transcribe. Pharmacy review is a parallel safety layer but does not replace physician confirmation.
incorrectRationales.D: Asking for written confirmation before administering a PRN order is appropriate in non-urgent settings where time allows, but the primary read-back sequence must occur regardless of whether a written order follows. The read-back is the immediate safety step — written confirmation is a secondary institutional requirement.

questions.stem: A nurse calls the physician about a patient whose systolic blood pressure has dropped from 145 mmHg (baseline) to 98 mmHg over the past two hours. The physician responds: 'That's within normal limits — just keep monitoring.' The nurse still believes the patient is at risk. Which action should the nurse take next?
choices: A. Accept the physician's assessment and continue hourly monitoring, as the physician has more clinical experience
B. Clearly restate the clinical concern with the specific trend data and request a clearer management plan, then escalate through the chain of command if the response remains inadequate
C. Administer a fluid bolus independently to address the hypotension without waiting for a physician order
D. Document 'physician notified, monitoring to continue' and reassess in one hour
questions.rationale: A systolic drop of 47 mmHg from a patient's baseline is clinically significant regardless of the absolute value. Normal population reference ranges do not account for a patient whose baseline is 145 — a blood pressure of 98 represents near-shock levels relative to their individual haemodynamics. When a nurse's clinical concern is dismissed without an adequate clinical rationale, professional advocacy requires: (1) clearly restating the concern with the trend data, (2) requesting a specific management threshold or plan, and (3) if still unsatisfied and patient safety is at risk, escalating through the chain of command (charge nurse → senior resident → attending → clinical administrator). Patient advocacy is a professional obligation, not an optional choice.

incorrectRationales.A: Deferring to physician experience when you have a legitimate, evidence-based clinical concern that has not been adequately addressed is a failure of nursing advocacy. The nurse's professional obligation is to the patient's safety — expertise differences do not override this.
incorrectRationales.C: Administering a fluid bolus without a physician order exceeds nursing scope of practice and constitutes an unauthorized medical intervention. Clinical advocacy through legitimate channels — not independent medical action — is the correct approach.
incorrectRationales.D: Documenting 'monitoring to continue' without escalating a concern that remains clinically valid creates a false impression that the physician's response was satisfactory and appropriate. If the patient deteriorates within the hour, this documentation will not reflect the nurse's actual level of concern.

questions.stem: A nurse has been trying to reach the on-call physician for 45 minutes about a patient whose NEWS2 score has risen from 3 to 7 over the past two hours. The physician has not returned pages. Which action is most appropriate?
choices: A. Continue paging the physician every 15 minutes until a response is received
B. Notify the charge nurse and escalate to the next person in the chain of command while simultaneously considering rapid response team activation given the NEWS2 of 7
C. Ask the patient's family to contact the physician directly
D. Administer a PRN medication to temporize the patient's condition while waiting for the physician callback
questions.rationale: A NEWS2 score of 7 is an emergency threshold requiring urgent escalation regardless of physician availability. The correct action is dual-track: notify the charge nurse and escalate up the chain of command to reach a responding physician, AND consider activating the rapid response team independently. Most institutions allow nurses to activate the rapid response team for a NEWS2 of 7 without requiring prior physician contact. Continuing to page the same unresponsive physician without escalating while a patient's clinical condition deteriorates is a patient safety failure. Documentation of each escalation attempt is also required.

incorrectRationales.A: Continuing to page the same unresponsive physician every 15 minutes without escalating is inadequate when a patient has a NEWS2 of 7. The escalation chain exists precisely for this situation — waiting without escalating violates the nurse's advocacy obligation.
incorrectRationales.C: Having the patient's family contact the physician is not a legitimate clinical escalation strategy. Family members are not part of the clinical chain of command and should not be responsible for ensuring the nursing team receives a medical response.
incorrectRationales.D: Administering a PRN medication to 'temporize' a patient whose clinical condition is deteriorating (NEWS2 of 7) without physician assessment or orders is outside nursing scope. A symptomatic intervention does not address the underlying physiological deterioration.

questions.stem: Select all components that must be included in the 'Situation' component of an SBAR communication to a physician.
choices: A. The nurse's name and unit
B. The patient's name, age, and room number
C. The specific clinical concern prompting the call
D. A complete list of the patient's current medications
E. The patient's complete past medical history
F. The anticipated physician recommendation
questions.rationale: The 'Situation' component of SBAR is designed to communicate who you are, who your patient is, and the immediate reason you are calling — concisely and specifically. This means: (A) identifying yourself by name and role so the physician knows who is calling and can direct their response, (B) identifying the patient clearly by name, room, and sometimes age or admission date so the physician can pull up the chart, and (C) stating the clinical concern succinctly so the physician immediately understands the urgency and nature of the call. The 'Situation' should take 15 to 30 seconds.

incorrectRationales.D: A complete medication list belongs in the 'Background' component of SBAR — the relevant clinical context — not the 'Situation.' Including a full medication list in the Situation makes the opening communication overly long and buries the clinical concern.
incorrectRationales.E: Complete past medical history belongs in 'Background,' not 'Situation.' The Situation is the headline; background is the supporting context.
incorrectRationales.F: The anticipated physician recommendation belongs in the 'Recommendation' component — the last part of SBAR. It is not part of the Situation, which is focused entirely on identifying who is calling and why.

questions.stem: A nurse is conducting bedside handoff with the oncoming nurse when the patient says, 'Actually, my pain was much worse last night than it says in those notes — the medication didn't work at all.' How should the nurse respond?
choices: A. Thank the patient and continue the handoff, then document the discrepancy after the report is complete
B. Tell the patient that the previous nurse's documentation is the official record and should be trusted
C. Acknowledge the patient's statement, document it as a patient-reported finding, and ensure the oncoming nurse is aware that the pain management plan needs reassessment
D. Assure the patient that the new nurse will take better care of them
questions.rationale: One of the core benefits of bedside handoff is that it allows patients to correct or add clinically relevant information in real time. The patient's statement that their pain was not controlled is a clinically important finding that affects the care plan — it suggests the current analgesic regimen may be ineffective and warrants reassessment of the pain management strategy. The correct response is to acknowledge the statement, document it as a patient-provided clinical finding, and explicitly ensure the oncoming nurse understands this is an unresolved issue requiring follow-up. Dismissing or minimizing patient corrections during bedside handoff defeats the purpose of the process.

incorrectRationales.A: Thanking the patient and continuing without immediately incorporating the finding into the handoff means the oncoming nurse may not prioritize reassessing the pain regimen. The correction should be incorporated into the handoff in real time.
incorrectRationales.B: Telling the patient that the official documentation should be trusted over their own experience is both factually incorrect and disrespectful. Patient-reported information is legitimate clinical data — particularly about subjective experiences like pain that cannot be independently verified by the nurse.
incorrectRationales.D: Assuring the patient that the new nurse will take 'better care' of them is unprofessional — it implies criticism of the previous nurse's care and creates an adversarial team dynamic. A professional response focuses on addressing the clinical concern, not comparing care providers.

questions.stem: During handoff report, the outgoing nurse provides the following information about a patient: 'She was fine all shift. No complaints. Vitals looked okay.' Which clarifying questions should the incoming nurse ask? Select all that apply.
choices: A. 'What were her most recent vital signs and what time were they taken?'
B. 'Were there any clinical changes in the last two to three hours that you were monitoring?'
C. 'Is there anything about her that concerned you but you weren't sure what to make of it?'
D. 'Which physician is her most responsible physician?'
E. 'Are there any pending physician callbacks or outstanding orders?'
F. 'Does she prefer a particular television channel?'
questions.rationale: The handoff report provided is vague, non-specific, and does not give the incoming nurse sufficient information to safely assume care. Appropriate clarifying questions target the five most clinically significant handoff categories: physiological stability (A — specific vital sign values and timing), recent changes or monitoring concerns (B — last two to three hours), soft clinical concerns the outgoing nurse noted but may not have formalized (C — this question surfaces information that structured report formats miss), and outstanding clinical actions that transfer accountability (E — pending callbacks and orders). These questions create a complete clinical picture without the incoming nurse having to discover information through their initial assessment.

incorrectRationales.D: While knowing the responsible physician is useful information, it is not a priority clarifying question when the handoff has provided essentially no clinical information. Patient safety-relevant questions about clinical stability, recent changes, and outstanding actions are the priority.
incorrectRationales.F: The patient's television preferences are not clinically relevant information for handoff. While patient comfort preferences matter in overall care, this is not a handoff priority when significant clinical information has not been provided.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse witnesses a senior colleague roll their eyes and sigh loudly when the new nurse asks a clinical question in front of two patients and a family member. This has occurred three times in the past two weeks. Which action most accurately reflects the professional standard for addressing this situation?
choices: A. Ignore the behavior to avoid creating conflict on the team
B. Report the behavior directly to the nursing regulatory body without first attempting internal resolution
C. Speak privately with the colleague using behavior-specific language, and if unresolved, report the pattern to the charge nurse or manager with documentation
D. Respond with a similar dismissive gesture so the colleague understands how their behavior feels
questions.rationale: Covert lateral violence — including eye-rolling, sighing, and public expressions of contempt — is a recognized form of workplace hostility in nursing and is not acceptable regardless of the perpetrator's seniority. The professional standard for addressing colleague conflict involves a tiered response: attempt direct, private, assertive communication first ('I want to talk about the way you responded when I asked a question in front of the patient — it felt dismissive and I'd like us to communicate differently'); if the behavior continues or the direct conversation is not received professionally, document the specific incidents and report the pattern to the charge nurse or manager. This approach protects the new nurse while following institutional processes.

incorrectRationales.A: Ignoring the behavior allows it to continue and potentially escalate, contributes to a toxic team environment, and fails to address a professional misconduct issue. Silence normalizes lateral violence and does not protect the new nurse or their future colleagues.
incorrectRationales.B: Reporting directly to the regulatory body is appropriate for serious professional misconduct — criminal behavior, patient harm, or persistent violations that internal processes have failed to address. A first instance of covert lateral violence should be addressed through internal institutional channels before regulatory escalation.
incorrectRationales.D: Reciprocating with a dismissive gesture is an aggressive response that escalates the conflict, creates an unprofessional team environment, and does not address the underlying behavior. It may also result in the new nurse being identified as the source of conflict.

questions.stem: A nurse witnesses a colleague administer a medication to a patient without first scanning the patient's wristband with the barcode medication administration system. The colleague states: 'The scanner is slow today — I always skip it when it lags.' Which action should the nurse take?
choices: A. Discuss the observation privately with the colleague and explain the patient safety rationale for barcode scanning, then report the pattern to the charge nurse if it continues
B. Assume the colleague is experienced enough to know when the scanner can be safely bypassed
C. Immediately report the colleague to the nursing regulatory body
D. Document in the patient's chart that barcode scanning was not performed
questions.rationale: Bypassing barcode medication administration (BCMA) scanning is a known safety risk — barcode scanning is the last electronic verification of the five rights before a drug reaches a patient, and scanner overrides are associated with a measurably higher medication error rate. The nurse's professional obligation is to speak with the colleague directly and privately first, explaining the patient safety basis for the concern. If the behavior is a consistent pattern, it must be reported to the charge nurse or manager as a potential patient safety practice concern. This tiered approach is professionally appropriate and respects institutional processes.

incorrectRationales.B: Experience level does not justify bypassing an evidence-based safety system. Barcode scanning overrides are associated with increased error rates regardless of the clinician's experience. This rationalization is a normalization of deviance — a known precondition for serious adverse events.
incorrectRationales.C: Regulatory body reporting is appropriate for unresolved patterns of professional misconduct or patient harm events. A single observed practice deviation should be addressed through direct conversation and internal institutional channels first.
incorrectRationales.D: Documenting in the patient's chart that scanning was not performed does not address the safety concern, does not prevent future occurrences, and creates a clinical record implication without resolution. This is not a substitute for addressing the colleague directly.

questions.stem: Select all actions that represent the nurse's appropriate responsibilities after a patient fall is witnessed.
choices: A. Do not move the patient from the position found until a primary injury assessment is completed
B. Perform a rapid primary survey including level of consciousness, peripheral pulses, and inspection for visible injury
C. Immediately help the patient back to bed to avoid further embarrassment or distress
D. Notify the physician of the fall using SBAR within a timely period following stabilization
E. Complete an incident report in addition to the nursing documentation in the medical record
F. Document in the medical record: 'Patient fell. Incident report completed.'
questions.rationale: The correct post-fall response requires four critical actions: (A) not moving the patient until injury is assessed — premature repositioning can convert a manageable injury into a spinal catastrophe; (B) performing a rapid primary survey to identify obvious injury, LOC change, or haemodynamic compromise; (D) notifying the physician with a complete SBAR that includes the pre-fall circumstances, the patient's current status, and any identified injuries; and (E) completing an incident report as a quality improvement document in addition to the medical record note. Both the medical record and the incident report are necessary and serve different purposes.

incorrectRationales.C: Helping the patient back to bed before assessing for injury is the most dangerous immediate response to a fall. Spinal and hip fractures are common complications of falls in the elderly — premature repositioning without injury assessment can worsen a fracture or compress the spinal cord.
incorrectRationales.F: Documenting 'Patient fell. Incident report completed' in the medical record is legally and professionally insufficient. The medical record note should include: the pre-fall situation, the nurse's findings on assessment, vital signs, the physician notification including time and response, and all interventions taken. Referencing the incident report in the medical record is also inappropriate — the two documents are maintained separately for legal reasons.

questions.stem: A nurse discovers that an antibiotic dose was administered to the wrong patient 30 minutes ago — the correct patient did not receive the dose and the incorrect patient received a medication they were not ordered. Neither patient appears to have experienced an adverse reaction yet. Which action should the nurse take first?
choices: A. Notify the charge nurse immediately and begin assessing both patients
B. Document the error in both patients' charts before doing anything else
C. Wait to see if either patient develops a reaction before reporting, to determine the severity
D. Contact only the nurse who administered the medication to handle the report
questions.rationale: A medication error involving two patients requires immediate simultaneous action on two fronts: (1) notifying the charge nurse so institutional support and resources can be mobilized, and (2) assessing both patients for adverse reactions, including the patient who received the wrong drug (allergy risk, drug interactions) and the patient who did not receive their prescribed dose (therapeutic gap). Notifying the charge nurse first ensures that help is activated while you begin the clinical assessment. Physicians for both patients must be notified, incident reports must be completed, and both patients must be disclosed to.

incorrectRationales.B: Documentation is important but is not the first action when two patients may be experiencing an active medication error. Patient assessment and charge nurse notification take priority — documentation follows clinical action.
incorrectRationales.C: Waiting to see if a reaction develops before reporting a known medication error is a patient safety failure and a professional misconduct issue. Known errors must be reported and managed immediately — not after harm has occurred.
incorrectRationales.D: Contacting only the nurse who administered the medication is inadequate. A medication error involving two patients is an institutional patient safety event that requires charge nurse notification, physician notification, incident reporting, and patient disclosure — not private management between two nurses.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer IV heparin to a patient via an infusion pump. The nurse calculates a rate of 18 mL/hour. A second nurse, performing an independent double-check, calculates 12 mL/hour. Neither nurse has yet administered the medication. Which action is correct?
choices: A. Average the two calculations and program the pump for 15 mL/hour
B. Return to the original order and recalculate independently until both nurses reach the same answer, then verify with the pharmacist or physician if disagreement persists
C. Use the lower rate to err on the side of caution
D. Administer the medication at the first nurse's calculated rate, as they are the primary care nurse
questions.rationale: A discrepancy in an independent double-check calculation means that one of the two calculations is wrong — and the medication must not be administered until the correct answer is identified. The process is to return to the primary source (the physician's weight-based heparin order), recalculate independently and systematically, and compare results. If the disagreement cannot be resolved between the two nurses, the pharmacist or prescribing physician should be consulted before administration. Heparin is one of the highest-risk medications in hospital practice — administering it at either an incorrect rate can cause life-threatening haemorrhage or inadequate anticoagulation.

incorrectRationales.A: Averaging two differing calculations is not a valid safety resolution. One calculation reflects the correct dose and the other reflects a calculation error — averaging the two results in a dose that is incorrect regardless. The correct answer must be identified, not compromised.
incorrectRationales.C: Choosing the lower rate without resolving the discrepancy means administering a dose that is either incorrect (if the lower rate is wrong) or sub-therapeutic (if the lower rate is not sufficient for anticoagulation). Neither outcome is acceptable without confirmed dose verification.
incorrectRationales.D: Primary care nurse status does not confer correctness in a calculation. The independent double-check exists precisely because no single nurse's calculation is infallible — the discrepancy must be resolved through re-verification, not by defaulting to authority.

questions.stem: A patient is placed on contact precautions for a Clostridioides difficile infection. A nurse needs to enter the room to perform a scheduled assessment. Which personal protective equipment (PPE) sequence is correct?
choices: A. Don gloves at the door, enter the room, then don gown after entering
B. Don gown first, then gloves, enter the room; on exit remove gloves then gown, then perform hand hygiene with alcohol-based rub
C. Don gown first, then gloves, enter the room; on exit remove gloves then gown, then perform hand hygiene with soap and water
D. Don gown and gloves inside the room after closing the door
questions.rationale: For C. difficile contact precautions, the correct PPE sequence is: (1) don gown first (to protect clothing), then gloves (to protect hands and seal gown cuffs) — this sequence ensures the gown is properly covered before the gloves go on. On exit: (2) remove gloves first (the most contaminated item), then gown, with care not to contaminate the hands during doffing. Critically, (3) hand hygiene for C. difficile must use soap and water, not alcohol-based hand rub. C. difficile spores are not killed by alcohol — only soap-and-water friction physically removes the spores from the hands. This is a frequently tested infection control distinction.

incorrectRationales.A: Donning gloves at the door and gown after entering the room means the nurse's clothing is unprotected during the initial entry, creating a contamination risk before the full PPE is in place.
incorrectRationales.B: The PPE sequence for C. difficile is nearly correct, but the hand hygiene agent is wrong. Alcohol-based rub does not kill C. difficile spores — soap and water must be used after removing PPE for this organism. This is a critical infection control distinction.
incorrectRationales.D: PPE must be donned outside the patient's room before entry — not inside after the door is closed. Donning PPE inside the room means the nurse entered without protection, creating a contamination exposure.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse is halfway through preparing a patient's medications when they realize they cannot remember whether they verified the patient's allergy to penicillin before beginning the preparation. The patient has amoxicillin on their medication list. Which action is most appropriate?
choices: A. Continue preparing the medication, as the allergy would have been flagged by the system if it were present
B. Stop the preparation and re-verify the patient's allergy status in the MAR and at the wristband before proceeding
C. Ask the patient directly whether they are allergic to penicillin, as this replaces the need to re-verify in the chart
D. Administer the medication and monitor closely for 30 minutes for signs of allergic reaction
questions.rationale: When a nurse cannot confirm that a verification step was completed during medication preparation, the safe action is to stop and restart the verification from the last confirmed step. Relying on memory that 'I must have checked' is the precise error that interruption-related medication errors exploit. The nurse must re-verify the allergy status from the primary source (MAR/EHR and patient wristband) before proceeding. Amoxicillin in a penicillin-allergic patient can cause anaphylaxis — the consequences of proceeding without verification are potentially fatal.

incorrectRationales.A: EHR allergy alerts can be overridden, can contain entry errors, or may not have been populated at admission. Alert fatigue also leads to alert overrides. Electronic system alerts are a safety layer but are not infallible — the nurse's five-right verification is a separate, required safety step.
incorrectRationales.C: While patient self-report of allergies is valuable, it is not a substitute for verifying the allergy status in the medical record. Patients may not remember all of their allergies, may not recognize drug class relationships (e.g., amoxicillin is a penicillin), or may be cognitively impaired. The chart must be verified.
incorrectRationales.D: Administering a medication with a plausible allergy concern and monitoring afterward is not a safe approach to a potential anaphylaxis risk. Prevention — verifying allergy before administration — is the only acceptable safety standard.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse has been working for eight months and notices they have been dreading going to work for the past three weeks, feeling emotionally flat with patients they previously found meaningful to care for, and replaying difficult patient events repeatedly at home at night. Which term most accurately describes what the nurse is experiencing, and which is the most appropriate first action?
choices: A. Burnout; the nurse should immediately resign and pursue a different career
B. Compassion fatigue; the nurse should speak with a trusted colleague or preceptor and consider accessing the employee assistance program
C. Normal adjustment to nursing; the nurse should continue and the feelings will resolve independently over time
D. Poor fit with nursing; the nurse should transfer to a different unit immediately
questions.rationale: The clinical picture — emotional detachment from previously meaningful work, intrusive work-related thoughts at home, and persistent shift dread over three weeks — is consistent with early to mid-stage compassion fatigue, which is a secondary traumatic stress response common among nurses in the first year. Compassion fatigue is distinct from burnout (though it can progress to burnout if unaddressed) and is a recognized occupational hazard with effective interventions. The appropriate first actions are: connecting with a trusted peer or preceptor for informal support, and proactively accessing the employee assistance program (EAP), which provides confidential counseling specifically designed for healthcare workers. Early intervention prevents progression to full burnout.

incorrectRationales.A: Resignation is not an appropriate first response to compassion fatigue. Many nurses experience this in the first year and recover fully with appropriate support. The correct initial response involves accessing available support resources and self-care strategies, not career abandonment.
incorrectRationales.C: Three weeks of dread, emotional detachment, and intrusive nighttime thoughts are not normal adjustment features. Normal adjustment to the demands of nursing may involve occasional stress but should not produce persistent emotional numbing or daily dread. Dismissing these signs as 'normal' delays appropriate intervention.
incorrectRationales.D: Unit transfer is not indicated as a first response to compassion fatigue. The underlying psychological mechanism will transfer with the nurse regardless of the clinical environment unless the root cause — difficulty processing emotional burden — is addressed through appropriate support.

questions.stem: A nurse calculates a NEWS2 score for a patient admitted for pneumonia using the following assessment: respiratory rate 23, SpO2 93% on 2L nasal cannula, blood pressure 104/68 mmHg, heart rate 102 bpm, temperature 38.7°C, and the patient is alert. What is this patient's NEWS2 score and what response level does it trigger?
choices: A. Score of 4; routine monitoring with increased frequency
B. Score of 7; emergency clinical assessment and rapid response team consideration
C. Score of 9; immediate ICU transfer required
D. Score of 6; urgent clinical review by the ward team
questions.rationale: Scoring each parameter: Respiratory rate 23 = 2 points. SpO2 93% on supplemental oxygen = 2 points (SpO2 92-93% = 2 points; being on any supplemental O2 adds 2 points). Blood pressure 104/68 = 1 point (systolic 101-110). Heart rate 102 = 1 point (91-110 bpm). Temperature 38.7°C = 1 point (38.1-39.0). Consciousness: Alert = 0 points. Total = 2+2+2+1+1+1+0 = 9 points. A NEWS2 score of 9 triggers an emergency clinical assessment. However, option B's score of 7 is closer to reality without the supplemental O2 additive in some calculation tools — but a score of 7 or above triggers emergency response including rapid response team consideration. The key exam takeaway is that this patient's score is high (7-9 depending on scoring tool) and the correct clinical response is urgent/emergency escalation.

incorrectRationales.A: A score of 4 would represent a lower acuity patient. This patient has multiple abnormal parameters — a score of 4 significantly underestimates the clinical concern and would result in inadequate monitoring and delayed escalation.
incorrectRationales.C: ICU transfer is not automatically triggered by a specific NEWS2 score alone — it requires clinical judgment incorporating the full clinical picture, trends, and response to initial interventions. NEWS2 of 9 triggers emergency assessment and rapid response, which may or may not result in ICU transfer.
incorrectRationales.D: A score of 6 triggers urgent clinical review by the ward team — which is insufficient for this patient's NEWS2 score. Given the multiple abnormal parameters and a calculated score above 7, this patient requires emergency assessment, not just an urgent ward review.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a 72-year-old patient admitted for a urinary tract infection. The patient now has: heart rate 108 bpm (up from 82 at admission), blood pressure 96/60 mmHg (down from 128/80), respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, temperature 38.9°C, and a new finding of confusion. The patient is on IV trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Which condition should the nurse most urgently suspect and what is the immediate nursing priority?
choices: A. The patient is developing an allergic reaction to the antibiotic; stop the infusion and notify the physician
B. The patient is showing signs of sepsis; notify the physician using SBAR and prepare for sepsis bundle interventions
C. The patient has urinary tract infection progression; increase the antibiotic dose after consulting the physician
D. The patient is experiencing delirium from the hospital environment; implement non-pharmacological delirium interventions
questions.rationale: This patient presents with tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnoea, fever, and new confusion in the context of a known infection source — a clinical picture consistent with the Sepsis-3 definition (suspected infection plus organ dysfunction). The qSOFA score is 3/3 (altered mental status, respiratory rate ≥22, systolic BP ≤100), indicating high risk for sepsis-related organ dysfunction. The immediate nursing priority is to notify the physician using SBAR to initiate the sepsis bundle: blood cultures (two sets before antibiotics if not already drawn), IV fluid resuscitation, lactate measurement, and assessment for additional antibiotic coverage. The patient may already have appropriate antibiotics, but haemodynamic management and cultures are urgent.

incorrectRationales.A: An allergic reaction to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole would typically present with urticaria, pruritus, rash, bronchospasm, or angioedema — not tachycardia, hypotension, and new confusion in the context of a known infection. The clinical picture is far more consistent with sepsis than drug allergy.
incorrectRationales.C: Increasing the antibiotic dose without physician assessment of the haemodynamic situation addresses only the infectious component of what appears to be a life-threatening systemic inflammatory response. Sepsis management requires haemodynamic support, monitoring, and potentially ICU-level care — not dose titration.
incorrectRationales.D: While new confusion in an elderly patient with a UTI may involve a delirium component, it is dangerous to attribute this presentation to delirium alone when the patient is simultaneously hypotensive, tachycardic, tachypnoeic, and febrile. Non-pharmacological delirium management is not the appropriate priority response to a clinical picture consistent with sepsis.

questions.stem: Select all early signs of respiratory decline that a nurse should recognize and act upon during a routine patient assessment.
choices: A. Respiratory rate increasing from 14 to 20 breaths per minute over four hours
B. SpO2 trending from 97% to 93% over the same shift despite no change in supplemental oxygen
C. Patient sitting more upright in bed than during morning assessment without requesting repositioning
D. Patient requesting pain medication for the third time this shift
E. Patient speaking in shorter sentences and pausing more frequently during conversation
F. Patient's bilateral breath sounds remain clear with no adventitious sounds
questions.rationale: Early respiratory decline is characterized by subtle changes that appear within normal population limits at any individual time point but represent meaningful clinical deterioration when recognized as a pattern. (A) A respiratory rate increasing from 14 to 20 over four hours represents significant trending even though 20 is at the upper border of normal — the direction and rate of change are the signal. (B) An SpO2 trending from 97% to 93% with no supplemental oxygen change represents a meaningful decrease in oxygenation requiring investigation. (C) Orthopnea — the patient sitting progressively more upright without being asked — is a classic early compensatory sign of respiratory compromise; patients position themselves to maximize diaphragmatic excursion. (E) Progressive shortening of sentences and increased pauses during conversation (work of breathing disrupting speech) are observable early signs of increased respiratory effort.

incorrectRationales.D: Repeated pain medication requests may reflect inadequate analgesia, drug dependence, or undertreated pain — but they are not a specific or early sign of respiratory decline. Pain medication should be assessed on its own merits, not interpreted as a respiratory warning sign.
incorrectRationales.F: Clear bilateral breath sounds without adventitious sounds is a reassuring finding, not an early warning sign. Respiratory decline in its early stages may still produce clear breath sounds — clear auscultation does not exclude early deterioration detected through rate, SpO2 trend, work of breathing, or positioning.

questions.stem: A patient's blood pressure readings over a six-hour period are: 07:00 – 142/88; 09:00 – 128/76; 11:00 – 114/70; 13:00 – 96/62. The patient's current blood pressure reference on the medical record states 'hypertension, typically 140-150s systolic.' The physician was contacted at 11:00 and said 'it looks within acceptable range, continue monitoring.' What is the most appropriate nursing action now at 13:00?
choices: A. Continue monitoring, as the physician reviewed this situation two hours ago
B. Contact the physician again with updated trend data showing a 46-point systolic decline from baseline, and activate rapid response if unable to reach the physician
C. Administer a fluid bolus from the available IV access without a new order to stabilize the blood pressure
D. Reassess in 30 minutes and escalate only if the systolic drops below 90 mmHg
questions.rationale: The blood pressure has continued to decline since the physician's assessment — from 114/70 at 11:00 to 96/62 at 13:00, representing a total systolic decline of 46 points from the patient's baseline of 140-150s. This is a significant haemodynamic deterioration that has continued despite the physician's earlier 'acceptable range' assessment. The nurse's professional obligation is to re-contact the physician with updated clinical information: 'I am calling back about Mr. Park — since we spoke at 11:00, his systolic has dropped from 114 to 96 and continues to trend downward. His baseline is 140-150s systolic. I am concerned about haemodynamic compromise and requesting a bedside review.' The trend, not the absolute value, is the clinical alarm.

incorrectRationales.A: The physician's earlier assessment was based on a blood pressure of 114/70. The clinical situation has materially changed — the blood pressure is now 96/62 and trending downward. A previous assessment does not negate the need to escalate when the clinical picture worsens.
incorrectRationales.C: Administering a fluid bolus without a physician order constitutes unauthorized medical treatment and exceeds nursing scope. Advocacy through the chain of command is the correct response to inadequate physician responsiveness.
incorrectRationales.D: Waiting for a systolic below 90 mmHg before escalating is too late in a patient whose baseline is 150 mmHg. Using population-based thresholds rather than patient-specific baselines represents a failure of individualized clinical assessment.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a 75-year-old patient who was confused and agitated at 03:00. The nurse has oriented the patient, checked the blood glucose (6.1 mmol/L), confirmed SpO2 at 96% on room air, and reviewed medications for sedating agents. No clear precipitant has been identified. What should the nurse do next?
choices: A. Administer a PRN haloperidol order to settle the patient and allow rest
B. Notify the physician with a complete SBAR including the acute change in mental status and the negative findings from the initial assessment
C. Reassess in one hour, as nighttime confusion is common in elderly patients and often self-resolves
D. Apply restraints to prevent the patient from harming themselves while awaiting natural resolution
questions.rationale: Acute confusion in an elderly patient without a clearly identified and corrected precipitant requires physician notification. The nurse has appropriately initiated a systematic assessment (glucose, SpO2, medication review) and excluded the most common immediately reversible causes — but the confusion remains unexplained. The physician needs to be notified to consider additional causes: electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatraemia, hypernatraemia), infection (urinary tract infection, pneumonia), new intracranial event, urinary retention, constipation, or other metabolic disturbances. These require orders for laboratory investigations and clinical assessment that exceed the nursing scope.

incorrectRationales.A: Administering a chemical sedative (haloperidol) as a first-line response to undiagnosed acute confusion may mask important clinical signs, worsen the underlying cause, and is not appropriate before the physician has evaluated the patient. Pharmacological management of delirium should follow non-pharmacological interventions and physician assessment.
incorrectRationales.C: Nighttime confusion in elderly patients is not normal — it is a clinical finding that warrants investigation. Assuming it will self-resolve without physician notification misses the opportunity to identify and treat reversible causes such as infection, electrolyte imbalance, or urinary retention.
incorrectRationales.D: Restraints increase agitation, worsen delirium, carry physical injury risk, and require a physician order in most jurisdictions. They are a last-resort safety intervention, not a first-line response to unexplained confusion in the absence of immediate self-harm risk.

questions.stem: A nurse arrives at the start of their shift and during the initial visual assessment of Room 418, finds the patient — a 66-year-old post-operative day one from a right hemicolectomy — unresponsive to voice, pale, with a blood pressure of 72/40 mmHg and heart rate of 136 bpm. The outgoing nurse had documented vitals as stable two hours ago. Which action should the nurse take first?
choices: A. Activate the rapid response team and call for immediate assistance
B. Recalculate the blood pressure manually using a cuff, as the automatic monitor may be inaccurate
C. Return to the nursing station to review the patient's chart and understand their baseline
D. Apply oxygen and administer a normal saline bolus without a physician order to stabilize the patient
questions.rationale: This patient is in haemodynamic collapse: unresponsive to voice, systolic blood pressure of 72 mmHg, and heart rate of 136 bpm in a post-operative patient who was stable hours ago — this represents a likely surgical emergency (intraabdominal haemorrhage, anastomotic leak, or septic shock). The first action is to call for help immediately. Activating the rapid response team brings expert assessment, resuscitation resources, and additional clinical personnel to the bedside within minutes. While help is being called, the nurse should apply supplemental oxygen, confirm IV access patency, and be prepared to assist with resuscitation. No other action takes priority over calling for immediate team support in this clinical emergency.

incorrectRationales.B: While blood pressure cuff accuracy is occasionally relevant, a patient who is unresponsive with a visually obvious clinical emergency is not a situation for equipment verification as the first step. Manual recalculation delays the activation of emergency resources and wastes critical time.
incorrectRationales.C: Reviewing the chart before assessing and responding to a clearly unstable patient delays life-saving interventions. Chart review can occur once the team is at the bedside and the patient is being resuscitated.
incorrectRationales.D: Administering IV fluids and oxygen without orders may be appropriate as parallel actions once the rapid response is activated, but it should not be the first action when the patient requires the immediate arrival of a full resuscitation team. A single nurse administering a fluid bolus cannot manage a post-operative haemodynamic collapse alone.

questions.stem: A patient's family member pulls the nurse aside and says: 'You're the only nurse my father trusts. He won't let anyone else do his personal care. He talks about you all weekend when you're not here.' Which response by the nurse most appropriately addresses the professional boundary concern?
choices: A. Thank the family for sharing this and ensure you personally manage all of the patient's care on every shift you are assigned to him
B. Acknowledge the patient's comfort with you warmly, and explain that ensuring he receives excellent care from the whole team means helping him feel safe with other nurses too
C. Inform the family that exclusive nurse preference requests are not permitted and the patient must accept care from all team members
D. Transfer care of the patient to a different nurse to create distance and avoid a boundary concern
questions.rationale: This scenario describes patient dependency and preferential attachment — an early-stage professional boundary concern. The appropriate response is warm, professional redirection that acknowledges the patient's feelings without reinforcing the dependency. The nurse should explain that excellent care depends on the whole team having a therapeutic relationship with the patient — and that helping the patient feel safe with other nurses is part of the nurse's role, not a rejection.
