This response is compassionate, maintains the nurse's professional accountability, and begins redirecting the relationship toward the team rather than reinforcing exclusive attachment.

incorrectRationales.A: Exclusively managing a patient's care to accommodate their preference for one nurse reinforces the dependency and violates therapeutic relationship principles. It makes care delivery logistically unsustainable and deepens the boundary concern rather than addressing it.
incorrectRationales.C: Informing the family that 'exclusive preferences are not permitted' is unnecessarily clinical and lacks compassion for the patient's vulnerability. The issue should be addressed through professional redirection, not a policy statement that may feel dismissive of the patient's emotional experience.
incorrectRationales.D: Immediately transferring the patient's care creates abrupt disruption that may cause distress to a patient who has formed an attachment, and it does not address the underlying dynamic. Professional redirection is the first appropriate response — care transfer is a last resort for severe boundary violations, not initial presentations of preferential attachment.

questions.stem: A nurse posts the following on a personal social media account after a difficult shift: 'Crazy day. Had a patient who came in for something routine and ended up crashing in the ICU. Unbelievable case. No names — just venting.' Which of the following statements about this post is accurate?
choices: A. The post is acceptable because no patient names were used
B. The post may constitute a privacy violation because describing a 'case' with enough detail for those who know the patient to identify them can constitute a breach of protected health information
C. The post is acceptable because it was posted on a personal account, not a work-related platform
D. The post is acceptable because the nurse explicitly stated they were 'just venting' and not disclosing specific medical information
questions.rationale: Under HIPAA (US) and PIPEDA (Canada), protected health information includes any information that could potentially be used to identify a patient — including descriptions of clinical events that, even without a name, may be identifiable to people who know the patient. 'A patient who came in for something routine and ended up crashing in the ICU' may be identifiable to family members, friends, or colleagues of the patient. The use of a personal account, the absence of a name, and the 'just venting' framing provide no legal or professional protection from a privacy violation. Nurses have a professional obligation to protect patient confidentiality absolutely — social media posts are not exempt.

incorrectRationales.A: The absence of a patient's name is insufficient to constitute privacy protection. Combinations of details — the type of admission, the clinical course, the institution — can be sufficient to identify a patient, particularly to people who know them.
incorrectRationales.C: The platform being personal rather than work-related provides no legal or professional protection. HIPAA, PIPEDA, and nursing codes of professional conduct apply to all settings in which a nurse communicates — not only work platforms.
incorrectRationales.D: The intent of the post (venting) is irrelevant to its legal and professional status. Privacy regulations are not intent-based — they are content-based. Patient information shared publicly is a privacy violation regardless of the poster's emotional state or stated purpose.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to perform a high-alert medication independent double-check for insulin with a colleague. The nurse asks: 'Which insulin did you calculate?' before completing their own calculation. Why is this a problem?
choices: A. It is not a problem — asking your colleague confirms the dose through verbal communication
B. This defeats the purpose of an independent double-check by introducing confirmation bias before each nurse completes an independent verification
C. Only one nurse needs to calculate the insulin dose; the second nurse only checks the pump rate
D. Verbal confirmation is sufficient for insulin without a formal double-check process
questions.rationale: An independent double-check requires each nurse to complete their verification separately before comparing results. When one nurse discloses their answer before the other has calculated, the second nurse's check is no longer independent — their calculation is cognitively influenced by the first nurse's answer (confirmation bias). This dramatically reduces the error-detection value of the double-check. The correct process is: each nurse calculates independently, then both nurses share their answers simultaneously or sequentially only after independent completion. If results match, the dose is confirmed. If they don't, the discrepancy must be resolved.

incorrectRationales.A: Verbal confirmation of a result does not constitute an independent verification. Independent means calculated separately without knowledge of the other's result. Asking for the answer before calculating converts an independent check into a co-dependent confirmation — a significantly weaker safety process.
incorrectRationales.C: An independent double-check for high-alert medications requires both nurses to independently verify all parameters: the drug, dose, concentration, rate, and clinical indication. Limiting the second nurse's role to pump rate only misses the most common source of insulin errors — dose calculation.
incorrectRationales.D: Insulin is a high-alert medication that requires a formal, documented independent double-check in most institutional policies. Verbal confirmation alone is not a substitute for an independent verification process and does not meet accreditation or safety standards.

questions.stem: A nurse has been assigned to a charge nurse who has told them they should 'stop asking so many questions' in front of the team. The new graduate nurse still has clinical uncertainty about a patient's care. Which response best reflects professional nursing practice?
choices: A. Stop asking questions in team settings and manage clinical uncertainty independently to comply with the charge nurse's expectation

B. Seek a private moment with the preceptor or charge nurse to discuss the clinical concern, and use alternative resources (physician, pharmacist, clinical resource nurse) when direct charge nurse access is limited
C. File a formal grievance immediately against the charge nurse for discouraging clinical questions
D. Post the clinical concern in the unit group chat to get a peer response
questions.rationale: Clinical uncertainty in a new graduate nurse requires appropriate resolution — never silent management. The charge nurse's comment about asking 'too many questions' may reflect a tone or setting concern rather than a prohibition on question-asking. The professional response is to seek a private conversation with the charge nurse to understand the concern while also identifying alternative resources for clinical consultation (the prescribing physician, clinical pharmacist, clinical resource nurse, or a trusted peer). Patient safety requires that clinical questions be asked and answered — the setting or format of the question can be adjusted, but the question must be asked.

incorrectRationales.A: Managing clinical uncertainty independently without seeking consultation when the nurse is genuinely uncertain is a patient safety risk and a scope-of-practice violation. A new graduate nurse who stops asking questions out of embarrassment or compliance with an inappropriate expectation may practice beyond their actual competency level with serious consequences.
incorrectRationales.C: Immediate formal grievance filing is disproportionate for a verbal comment in a team setting. Professional escalation follows a tiered approach — address directly, then escalate through management channels, then formal processes if needed.
incorrectRationales.D: Posting a patient care concern in a unit group chat raises privacy concerns if any patient-identifying information is included, and may not reach the appropriate clinical resource for the specific question. Professional consultation should occur through appropriate, confidential channels.

questions.stem: A nurse discovers that a colleague has been routinely silencing telemetry alarms in a patient's room 'because the patient finds the sound annoying.' No documentation of the alarm parameter adjustments exists and the physician has not been notified. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
choices: A. Restore the telemetry alarm settings to the ordered parameters and ensure monitoring is active
B. Speak with the colleague privately about the patient safety rationale for alarm management
C. Report the practice to the charge nurse as a patient safety concern
D. Silence the alarms yourself, as the patient's preference for quiet should be accommodated
E. Complete an incident report documenting the practice as a patient safety near-miss
F. Ask the patient whether they want the alarms silenced before deciding what to do
questions.rationale: Routinely silencing monitoring alarms without physician orders, without parameter adjustments based on clinical justification, and without documentation represents a patient safety practice failure — this is alarm management that directly increases the risk of missed deterioration and is a recognized contributor to in-hospital cardiac arrest events. The appropriate responses are: (A) immediately restoring the correct monitoring to protect the current patient, (B) addressing the colleague directly with a clinical rationale, (C) reporting the pattern to the charge nurse as a safety concern affecting care quality, and (E) completing an incident report as a patient safety near-miss to trigger institutional review and system improvement.

incorrectRationales.D: Accommodating a patient's preference for quiet by silencing clinical alarms without clinical justification or orders is a patient safety violation. Patient comfort preferences must be balanced against safety monitoring requirements — and a physician must be involved in any decision to modify monitoring parameters.
incorrectRationales.F: Asking the patient whether they want alarms silenced places a clinical decision in the hands of a layperson. Patients do not have the clinical knowledge to make informed decisions about the safety implications of disabling their monitoring — this is a nursing and physician responsibility.

questions.stem: A nurse is working on a unit where the team routinely documents vital signs in the nursing notes before they are actually collected, filling in 'expected' values to save time. The charge nurse has normalized this practice and several experienced nurses participate. What is the most appropriate response from the new graduate nurse?
choices: A. Participate in the practice to fit in with the established team culture
B. Document only vital signs that were actually collected, and if pressured, escalate the concern through appropriate channels including the nurse manager or regulatory body
C. Document the expected values but add a note that they are estimates
D. Approach the charge nurse privately and suggest documenting vital signs 'approximately' rather than fabricating them
questions.rationale: Documenting vital signs that were not collected is falsification of the medical record — a serious professional and legal violation in both Canada and the United States. This practice is not mitigated by team consensus, charge nurse endorsement, or institutional normalization. A nurse who participates in documentation falsification is individually accountable for that entry, regardless of team culture. The correct response is to document only what was actually assessed. If the nurse is pressured to falsify documentation, they must escalate through appropriate channels: nurse manager, director of nursing, and if institutional channels fail to address the issue, the provincial or state nursing regulatory body.

incorrectRationales.A: Participating in documentation falsification to fit in with team culture is not an acceptable professional choice. Team culture cannot override professional regulatory standards and legal obligations. A nurse who falsifies documentation is personally accountable for those entries.
incorrectRationales.C: Documenting 'estimated' vital signs that were not collected is still falsification. The medical record must reflect actual clinical findings — estimation is not a legal or professional substitute for actual measurement.
incorrectRationales.D: Suggesting a more palatable form of falsification ('approximately') does not resolve the ethical and legal issue. The problem is not the precision of the values but the fact that they are being entered without an actual assessment. The solution is actual measurement, not a euphemistic label for fabricated data.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse has been working for six months and notices they frequently second-guess correct decisions after the fact, attribute positive patient outcomes to luck rather than their own clinical skill, and dread being asked clinical questions by more experienced nurses. Which of the following statements best characterizes this experience?
choices: A. These are signs of a significant competency deficit that requires formal remediation
B. This is a normal manifestation of imposter syndrome, which is common among new nursing graduates and can be managed through reflection and peer support
C. This experience indicates the nurse is not suited for acute care nursing and should consider a different specialty
D. This is a sign of compassion fatigue and the nurse should immediately take a leave of absence
questions.rationale: The clinical picture — second-guessing correct decisions, attributing successes to luck, and fear of being exposed as incompetent — is a textbook description of imposter syndrome. Research indicates that approximately 70% of people experience imposter syndrome at some point, and it is particularly prevalent during professional transitions. Importantly, imposter syndrome is defined by a discrepancy between the internal experience of competence and the external evidence of competence — it does not indicate a genuine competency deficit. The nurse is making correct decisions but attributing them to external factors. Management includes structured reflection, peer support, deliberate tracking of competency milestones, and normalization through mentorship.

incorrectRationales.A: Imposter syndrome is not a competency deficit — it is a cognitive pattern that distorts the interpretation of competence evidence. The nurse is described as making correct decisions, which contradicts a competency-based explanation. Formal remediation is not indicated.
incorrectRationales.C: Imposter syndrome is not specialty-specific and does not indicate poor career fit. Many highly effective nurses in acute care settings experience imposter syndrome in the first year. Career redirection based on imposter syndrome alone would deprive the profession of nurses who may become excellent practitioners.
incorrectRationales.D: The description does not meet the criteria for compassion fatigue, which is characterized by emotional numbing, vicarious trauma, avoidance of clinical work related to emotional exposure, and physical symptoms related to over-absorption of patient suffering. The nurse described is experiencing cognitive self-doubt, not secondary traumatic stress.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse wants to contribute a clinical observation during morning rounds when the attending physician, two residents, and three senior nurses are present. The nurse has noticed that the patient's urine output over the past eight hours was 180 mL — below the 0.5 mL/kg/hr threshold for a 70 kg patient. Using the SBAR framework for communication in a team setting, which opening statement most effectively contributes this observation?
choices: A. 'I'm not sure if this is important, but I was wondering about the urine output...'

B. 'I've been watching the urine output and noticed it's down to 180 mL over eight hours — below the 0.5 mL/kg/hr threshold for this patient's weight. I wanted to flag this as a possible early indicator of acute kidney injury or volume depletion.'
C. 'Someone should check the patient's urine output because I think it might be low.'
D. 'I'll let the physician decide about the urine output — they have more experience with this kind of finding.'
questions.rationale: A strong clinical voice uses specific, objective language to contribute a clinical finding with interpretive context. The statement in option B includes: the finding (180 mL over eight hours), the clinical standard it violates (0.5 mL/kg/hr for a 70 kg patient), and the clinical interpretation (possible early AKI or volume depletion). This is the Situation and Assessment portion of an SBAR delivered in a team setting. It is confident, specific, and invites clinical dialogue without being presumptuous. New graduates who use this kind of structured, evidence-based contribution are taken seriously and contribute meaningfully to patient care in team settings.

incorrectRationales.A: Opening with 'I'm not sure if this is important' pre-emptively discredits the observation and makes it easy for the team to dismiss. This hedging language is a common feature of imposter syndrome and diminishes clinical contributions that may be entirely valid.
incorrectRationales.C: Vague language ('I think it might be low') without specific data forces the team to investigate what the nurse could have already quantified. This approach is less effective and less credible than providing the specific clinical finding with context.
incorrectRationales.D: Deferring to the physician based on experience rather than contributing a specific, observed clinical finding represents a failure to use the nursing clinical voice. The nurse's direct observation of output trends is valuable data that should be shared — not withheld out of deference.

questions.stem: A nurse enters Room 412 to perform a first-assessment on a patient received in report as a 'stable, chronic COPD patient, no acute concerns.' The patient is found sitting upright at 90 degrees, using visible accessory muscles with each breath, with a respiratory rate of 28, SpO2 of 88% on 2L nasal cannula, and a heart rate of 118 bpm. The patient says 'I'm okay — just a bit tired.' Which action should the nurse take first?
choices: A. Accept the patient's self-assessment and proceed with the planned shift activities
B. Immediately apply a non-rebreather mask at 10-15 L/min, call for assistance, and initiate rapid response if the patient does not improve
C. Document the findings and contact the physician during the next rounding window
D. Ask the patient if they would like to increase their nasal cannula flow rate
questions.rationale: This patient is in moderate to severe respiratory distress: accessory muscle use, respiratory rate of 28, SpO2 of 88% on 2L supplemental oxygen, and compensatory tachycardia. This is not a presentation consistent with 'stable chronic COPD' — this is an acute exacerbation requiring immediate nursing intervention and escalation. The correct first action is to increase the oxygen delivery (applying a non-rebreather mask for higher FiO2), call for immediate help, and notify the charge nurse and activate rapid response if the SpO2 does not improve. The patient's own minimization ('I'm okay — just tired') reflects the adaptive tolerance that some COPD patients develop for chronic dyspnoea — it is not a reliable indicator of clinical safety.

incorrectRationales.A: Accepting the patient's self-assessment in the presence of objective signs of acute respiratory compromise is a clinical error. Patients with chronic lung disease often tolerate and minimize symptoms that represent a significant acute change from their physiological reserve — the nurse's objective assessment takes precedence.
incorrectRationales.C: Documenting the findings and waiting for the next rounding window is dangerously delayed escalation. An SpO2 of 88% with respiratory rate of 28 and visible accessory muscle use requires immediate clinical response — not deferred documentation.
incorrectRationales.D: Asking the patient if they would like to increase the nasal cannula flow rate delegates a clinical decision to a patient in distress and uses an inappropriate delivery mechanism. A nasal cannula provides maximum FiO2 of approximately 44% at 6L/min — insufficient for a patient with SpO2 of 88%. A non-rebreather mask is required for rapid oxygen supplementation in acute respiratory distress.

questions.stem: A nurse arrives for the night shift and receives report that includes the following patient in Room 308: an 80-year-old with a history of atrial fibrillation and mild cognitive impairment, admitted for a hip fracture, post-operative day two, blood pressure stable, SpO2 94% on room air. At 02:30, the patient is found in the hallway calling out for their husband (who is deceased). The patient is ambulatory and does not appear injured. Which action should the nurse take first?
choices: A. Restrain the patient to prevent them from wandering and assess in the morning
B. Calmly reorient the patient, guide them back to bed safely, and perform a focused assessment including level of consciousness, vital signs, and a pain assessment
C. Administer the ordered PRN lorazepam immediately to prevent the patient from falling
D. Notify the family by phone and ask them to come in to sit with the patient
questions.rationale: The nurse's immediate priority is to ensure the patient's physical safety by guiding them back to bed, and then to perform a focused assessment to determine whether this episode represents an expected sundowning pattern or a new acute change. The focused assessment should include level of consciousness compared to baseline (has cognition changed from what is documented?), vital signs, a pain assessment (post-op hip fracture patients are at high risk for undertreated pain triggering agitation), SpO2, and a brief medication review. This systematic approach follows safe delirium management principles: non-pharmacological first, identify and treat precipitants, escalate to physician if no identifiable cause is found.

incorrectRationales.A: Restraints increase agitation, worsen delirium, restrict circulation increasing DVT risk in a post-operative hip patient, and require a physician order in most jurisdictions. Restraints are a last resort — never a first response to a confused ambulatory patient.
incorrectRationales.C: Lorazepam and other benzodiazepines are contraindicated in elderly delirium management as they worsen confusion, increase fall risk, and can cause respiratory depression — particularly in older adults with atrial fibrillation who may be on other cardiac medications. Pharmacological sedation requires physician assessment and order.
incorrectRationales.D: Calling the family at 02:30 may be appropriate in some situations — particularly if the patient is significantly distressed and the family has requested involvement — but it is not the first nursing action. The nurse must first ensure the patient's immediate safety and perform a clinical assessment before involving the family.

questions.stem: A patient's family member tells the nurse: 'My mother has a living will that says she does not want CPR. She hasn't mentioned it to her doctors here, but I want to make sure everyone knows.' The patient is alert and oriented. Which action should the nurse take?
choices: A. Accept the family's statement and document 'Patient is DNR per family' in the chart
B. Ask the patient directly about their advance directive and code status preference, then notify the physician so a formal code status order can be placed
C. Request a copy of the advance directive from the family and place it on the nursing unit without physician involvement
D. Reassure the family that the information will be communicated at the next team meeting
questions.rationale: A code status or DNR order is a medical order that must be placed by a physician — it cannot be established through nurse documentation based on family report. Because the patient is alert and oriented, the nurse's first action is to confirm the patient's own wishes directly, then notify the physician to ensure a formal code status order is entered. Advance directives are legally important documents that should be reviewed by the medical team and formally incorporated into the plan of care. The family's report is a valuable clinical flag — but it must be confirmed with the patient and translated into a formal physician order.

incorrectRationales.A: Documenting 'DNR per family' without verifying with the patient (who is alert and oriented) and obtaining a physician order is both legally inadequate and potentially contrary to the patient's actual wishes. Family members cannot establish code status on behalf of a competent patient.
incorrectRationales.C: Placing an advance directive document on the unit without physician notification does not result in a medical order. The physician must review the directive, discuss it with the patient, and enter a formal code status order for it to be actionable in a resuscitation scenario.
incorrectRationales.D: Deferring code status communication to 'the next team meeting' when the family has raised an urgent end-of-life preference is a clinical communication failure. Code status should be addressed immediately and formally — not deferred to a scheduled meeting.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes about oral medication management. After the session, the nurse asks: 'Do you understand what we talked about?' The patient says 'Yes, of course.' Which documentation entry best reflects an adequate patient education encounter?
choices: A. 'Patient education provided regarding type 2 diabetes medication management. Patient verbalized understanding.'
B. 'Medication management education provided. Patient states she understands. Will reinforce at discharge.'

C. 'Metformin 500 mg BID education provided. Patient asked to demonstrate how she will take her medications at home — she correctly described taking them with meals, identified the importance of not skipping doses, and stated she would hold the medication and call her doctor if she developed vomiting or diarrhea that prevented eating. Written materials provided in English. No identified learning barriers.'
D. 'Patient educated on type 2 diabetes. Handout given. Understanding confirmed.'
questions.rationale: Complete patient education documentation must include: the specific content taught, the educational method used, a specific teach-back response (not just 'patient said yes'), the patient's actual demonstration of understanding, any written materials provided, identified learning barriers, and any follow-up plan. Option C meets all of these criteria: it specifies the medication, documents specific teach-back responses that demonstrate comprehension (taking with meals, not skipping doses, holding the medication with GI symptoms), records material provision, and identifies the absence of barriers. This is legally defensible, clinically useful, and demonstrates that learning actually occurred.

incorrectRationales.A: 'Verbalized understanding' in response to 'do you understand?' is not a teach-back evaluation. The closed yes/no question does not assess comprehension — it assesses the patient's willingness to say yes. This entry does not document what was taught, how it was evaluated, or what the patient actually demonstrated.
incorrectRationales.B: This entry is incomplete — it lacks specific content, a teach-back demonstration, written materials documentation, and learning barrier assessment. 'Will reinforce at discharge' is a follow-up plan without a documented current education outcome.
incorrectRationales.D: 'Understanding confirmed' based on what method? This entry is vague, undocumented in process, and does not meet the professional or legal standard for patient education documentation.

questions.stem: A nurse has just completed a code blue in which the patient did not survive. The nurse is visibly shaking and has three other patients to attend to. A colleague offers: 'I've got your patients for the next 20 minutes — go sit down.' Which action most reflects evidence-based post-traumatic event practice?
choices: A. Decline the offer and immediately return to patient care to maintain professional performance standards
B. Accept the offer, take 10 to 15 minutes to decompress (sit quietly, call a trusted person, breathe), and participate in a brief team debrief if one is offered before resuming care
C. Report to the nursing manager that you are unable to continue working and request early relief
D. Return to your patients and discuss the code events with each patient to process the experience
questions.rationale: Brief post-event decompression following a traumatic clinical event is an evidence-supported resilience strategy and is not a sign of professional weakness. A nurse who is visibly shaking immediately after a code is experiencing a physiological stress response — cortisol, adrenaline, and sympathetic activation — that temporarily impairs the clinical judgment and fine motor coordination needed for safe patient care. Accepting colleague support for a brief defined period, engaging in brief decompression, and participating in a team debrief (if offered) allows the physiological stress response to begin resolving before returning to clinical care. This protects both the nurse and their patients.

incorrectRationales.A: Immediately returning to clinical care while visibly shaking and without any decompression time creates patient safety risk. A nurse in an acute physiological stress response has temporarily impaired cognitive performance. Stoically returning without any recovery period is not professional — it is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.C: Reporting to the manager that you cannot continue and requesting early relief is appropriate only if the acute stress response does not resolve after brief decompression — which is a rare outcome. Most nurses can return to safe practice within 15 to 20 minutes with colleague support. Early escalation to relief should follow, not precede, a brief recovery opportunity.
incorrectRationales.D: Discussing a traumatic clinical event with patients is a professional boundary violation and is not appropriate. Processing clinical events is done with colleagues, preceptors, or mental health professionals — not with patients who are in a vulnerable position in your care.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse is conducting a shift debrief using the Rolfe reflective model after administering a medication to a patient who then became confused. The nurse identifies the 'What?' step as 'I administered hydromorphone 0.5 mg IV push at 14:30 for pain rated 8/10. Thirty minutes later, the patient was confused and responding slowly.' Which statement represents the best 'Now What?' reflection?
choices: A. 'I will never administer opioids without supervision again.'

B. 'Next time I administer an opioid to a patient over 70, I will perform a baseline cognitive and respiratory assessment before administration and reassess at 15 and 30 minutes, with particular attention to level of consciousness and respiratory rate.'
C. 'I should have used a lower dose regardless of the physician's order.'
D. 'This situation shows that opioids are generally unsafe for elderly patients and should be avoided.'
questions.rationale: The 'Now What?' step in the Rolfe model produces a specific, actionable, and clinically realistic behavioral change for future practice. Option B identifies the specific patient population (over 70), the specific clinical action (pre-administration baseline and structured post-administration reassessment), and the specific parameters to monitor (LOC, respiratory rate). This reflection is practical, evidence-based, and directly linked to the learning from the event. It improves future practice rather than creating avoidance or generalizing incorrectly.

incorrectRationales.A: 'Never administer opioids without supervision again' is an overcorrection that is impractical and not clinically supported. Opioids are appropriately prescribed and administered by nurses regularly. A blanket avoidance rule does not reflect the clinical learning — the learning is about systematic pre- and post-administration assessment in high-risk populations.
incorrectRationales.C: Modifying a medication dose independently from the physician's order is outside nursing scope. The learning from this event should be about enhanced assessment practice, not independent dose modification.
incorrectRationales.D: 'Opioids are generally unsafe for elderly patients' is a clinical generalization that does not accurately reflect evidence-based opioid pharmacology. Elderly patients do require dose adjustments and enhanced monitoring, but opioids are not categorically contraindicated in this population.

questions.stem: A patient with a known history of type 2 diabetes is admitted for a urinary tract infection. At 06:30 on the morning of the second day, the patient's breakfast tray arrives and the nurse prepares to administer insulin lispro 8 units subcutaneously as ordered. The patient reports they feel nauseous and 'don't think I can eat.' The blood glucose is 7.2 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate nursing action?
choices: A. Administer the insulin as ordered, as the blood glucose is elevated and the insulin is needed
B. Hold the insulin, assist the patient with a small amount of food or carbohydrate if tolerated, and contact the physician to clarify the insulin order for a patient who cannot eat
C. Administer half the ordered dose to account for the reduced meal intake
D. Skip the insulin entirely without notifying the physician, as the patient's glucose is only mildly elevated
questions.rationale: Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting insulin designed to cover carbohydrate intake at a meal. Administering it to a patient who cannot eat creates a high hypoglycaemia risk — the insulin will drive the blood glucose down rapidly without the expected meal-based glucose to offset it. With a blood glucose of 7.2 mmol/L and no meal intake, hypoglycaemia could occur within 30 to 60 minutes of administration. The correct action is to hold the rapid-acting insulin and contact the physician to clarify the insulin order. The physician may provide alternative instructions (basal insulin only, a reduced dose if some food is consumed, or IV dextrose if indicated).

incorrectRationales.A: Administering rapid-acting insulin to a patient who reports they cannot eat is a patient safety error. A blood glucose of 7.2 mmol/L combined with no anticipated carbohydrate intake creates a high hypoglycaemia risk within the drug's action window (peak at 30-90 minutes for insulin lispro).
incorrectRationales.C: Independently modifying an insulin dose from what is ordered (administering half without a physician order) is outside nursing scope. Any dose modification requires a new physician order. Nursing judgment guides whether to hold pending contact with the physician — it does not authorize dose adjustment.
incorrectRationales.D: Holding insulin without notifying the physician is not appropriate for a patient with insulin-requiring diabetes. The physician needs to know that the scheduled insulin was held and the clinical reason, so they can provide alternative management instructions and ensure glycaemic control is maintained through other means.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a 58-year-old patient admitted for chest pain workup who is now one hour into the shift. The patient was described in report as 'chest pain resolved, stable for observation.' During the assessment, the patient says 'I feel a bit funny — not sick, just... different.' Their vital signs are: heart rate 96, blood pressure 128/78, SpO2 96%, respiratory rate 18, temperature 36.8°C. The ECG from two hours ago was normal. What should the nurse do?
choices: A. Document 'patient reports feeling different, vitals stable, no intervention required' and continue planned care
B. Reassure the patient that feeling 'funny' is normal during cardiac monitoring and reassess in one hour
C. Perform a focused assessment, obtain a repeat 12-lead ECG, and notify the physician of the patient's new subjective complaint with full clinical context
D. Ask the patient to quantify their symptoms more specifically before deciding whether to escalate
questions.rationale: A patient admitted for chest pain who develops a new subjective complaint — even if non-specific — during the observation period warrants immediate assessment. The phrase 'I feel different' in a patient under cardiac monitoring is a clinical alarm until proven otherwise. Possible explanations include: an evolving arrhythmia, an early ischaemic event (which may produce prodromal symptoms before ECG or troponin changes), haemodynamic instability not yet captured in the static vital signs, or a non-cardiac cause. A repeat 12-lead ECG is the correct immediate diagnostic action for a patient with a cardiac workup diagnosis. The physician needs to be informed so that monitoring can be intensified and orders adjusted.

incorrectRationales.A: Documenting 'no intervention required' based on stable vital signs alone dismisses a new subjective complaint in a patient who is currently being evaluated for a cardiac event. Vital signs may be normal in the early stages of an ischaemic event — a new symptom demands investigation, not documentation without action.
incorrectRationales.B: Feeling 'funny' during cardiac monitoring is not normal and should not be normalized. Reassuring the patient without performing a clinical assessment and obtaining an ECG creates a risk of missing an evolving cardiac event during the very observation period designed to detect it.
incorrectRationales.D: Asking the patient to quantify their symptoms more precisely before deciding whether to escalate delays the clinical response. Some ischaemic events present with vague, non-specific symptoms that patients struggle to characterize precisely — this is a known feature of cardiac presentations, not a reason to defer assessment.

questions.stem: At the start of the shift a nurse receives the following patient assignment. Which patient should be assessed first?
choices: A. A 62-year-old with well-controlled type 2 diabetes requesting a blood glucose check before breakfast
B. A 77-year-old admitted for community-acquired pneumonia whose SpO2 was 91% on 2L nasal cannula per the previous night's last charted set, two hours ago
C. A 49-year-old who underwent an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy yesterday afternoon and is awaiting discharge instructions
D. A 55-year-old with hypertension asking the nurse when their next antihypertensive dose is due
questions.rationale: The patient with pneumonia and a documented SpO2 of 91% two hours ago represents the most acute physiological concern and should be assessed first. An SpO2 of 91% is below the clinically acceptable threshold of 92-94% in a patient on supplemental oxygen, and the value is two hours old — the patient's current status is unknown. The nurse cannot determine from the available information whether the SpO2 has improved, remained the same, or continued to decline. This patient represents an unresolved 'B' (Breathing) concern on the ABCDE framework and is the most physiologically vulnerable in this assignment. All other patients have either stable chronic conditions or post-operative presentations without acute respiratory compromise.

incorrectRationales.A: A blood glucose check before breakfast in a stable diabetic patient is a Tier 2 task — important and time-limited by meal delivery, but it does not represent an immediate physiological threat comparable to a low SpO2 in a pneumonia patient.
incorrectRationales.C: A patient awaiting discharge instructions after an elective laparoscopic procedure who is stable is a Tier 3 task — clinically non-urgent and safely deferred until after the acute assessment priorities are addressed.
incorrectRationales.D: A question about medication timing from a hypertensive patient is a Tier 3 inquiry. It can be answered during the medication round or delegated to a colleague, and it carries no immediate physiological urgency.

questions.stem: A nurse enters a patient's room and finds the patient sitting at the bedside with their IV catheter completely dislodged — there is a small pool of blood on the floor. The patient says they tried to get up to use the washroom themselves and the IV caught on the bedrail. The patient is on telemetry and their current heart rate is 88 bpm and blood pressure 122/78. They have an order for 2 mg IV hydromorphone PRN which was due to be given in 20 minutes for pain management. What is the correct order of nursing actions?
choices: A. Apply pressure to the IV site to stop bleeding, assess the site, establish new IV access, then reassess the pain management plan with the physician
B. Administer the hydromorphone orally as a substitute, then address the IV site
C. Call the physician immediately about the lost IV access before providing any direct patient care
D. Document the IV dislodgement in the incident report before providing care to preserve the timeline
questions.rationale: The correct prioritization is: (1) patient safety — apply direct pressure to the IV site to stop any ongoing bleeding; (2) assess — inspect the IV site, skin integrity, and volume of blood loss; (3) restore access — establish new IV access, as the patient has a PRN IV opioid order that requires IV access; (4) communicate and replan — notify the physician that IV access was lost and discuss the timing and route of the next hydromorphone dose. This sequence follows the principle of direct patient care first, then administrative and communicative actions. The patient is haemodynamically stable, so the bleeding site management and IV re-establishment are the priority before any notifications.

incorrectRationales.B: Hydromorphone IV and hydromorphone oral are not equivalent routes or doses — oral administration of an IV-formulated drug is not appropriate. Route substitution for an opioid requires a new physician order specifying the route, dose, and frequency. The nurse cannot independently convert IV to oral opioids.
incorrectRationales.C: Calling the physician before providing direct patient care when a patient has an active bleeding IV site is an incorrect prioritization. The physical safety of the patient — stopping the bleeding — is the first nursing action. The physician notification follows stabilization.
incorrectRationales.D: Completing an incident report before providing patient care is never the correct sequence. Incident reports are completed after patient safety has been established, care has been provided, and the physician has been notified.

questions.stem: A nurse manager observes that a staff nurse has been consistently 30 to 45 minutes late completing shift documentation over the past three weeks, often staying late unpaid and appearing frustrated. The nurse's patient assessments and medication administration are completed on time, but narrative notes are consistently delayed. Which statement best describes the probable underlying issue and the manager's most appropriate initial response?
choices: A. The nurse is demonstrating poor time management skills and should be placed on a performance improvement plan

B. The nurse's clinical priorities (assessment and medications) appear to be correctly ordered; the documentation workflow may need optimization, and the manager should have a supportive conversation to explore workload challenges
C. The nurse should be required to complete all documentation within 15 minutes of each clinical event or face disciplinary action
D. The delayed documentation is a patient safety concern and the nurse should be reported to the regulatory body
questions.rationale: A nurse who completes clinical assessments and medications on time but consistently struggles with documentation timing is likely prioritizing correctly (patients first, documentation second) but may lack an efficient documentation workflow or face a workload that genuinely exceeds what the shift allows. The manager's first response should be a supportive, exploratory conversation: are there specific documentation barriers (EHR usability, workload, unclear expectations)? Is the nurse working through a patient acuity challenge? Is this a skill issue (documentation efficiency) or a systems issue (inadequate time for the workload)? Punitive responses before understanding the cause are both premature and likely counterproductive.

incorrectRationales.A: A performance improvement plan as a first response to consistently delayed documentation, without exploring the cause, is premature and potentially inappropriate if the underlying issue is systemic (workload, EHR barriers, patient acuity). Performance improvement plans are appropriate when there is a persistent pattern that supportive management has not resolved.
incorrectRationales.C: Requiring documentation within 15 minutes of each clinical event without understanding the workflow context is an arbitrary and potentially unachievable standard in a busy clinical setting. This approach addresses symptoms without addressing causes.
incorrectRationales.D: Delayed narrative documentation — when all clinical care has been provided and documented in flowsheets — is not a regulatory-level patient safety concern without evidence of harm. Regulatory reporting is disproportionate as an initial response to a documentation workflow problem.

questions.stem: A family member of a patient in the ICU step-down unit says to the nurse: 'My mother told me she doesn't want any more blood transfusions, but her hemoglobin is dropping and I think she needs one. She's been confused today and doesn't know what she's saying.' The patient's chart documents no formal advance directive regarding transfusions and the patient was alert and oriented at the beginning of the shift. The patient is currently mildly confused. Which action should the nurse take?
choices: A. Accept the family's interpretation and proceed without transfusion, as the patient has expressed wishes against it
B. Administer the transfusion based on the clinical indication and family consent, as the patient is currently confused
C. Assess the patient's current level of decision-making capacity, document the finding, and notify the physician to facilitate an appropriate informed consent or substitute decision-making process
D. Contact the ethics committee before taking any action
questions.rationale: This scenario involves a potential conflict between a patient's previously expressed wishes (family-reported refusal of transfusions) and a clinical decision (transfusion for dropping haemoglobin) in the context of a patient whose current decision-making capacity is in question due to confusion. The correct nursing action is to assess the patient's current capacity — does the patient understand their situation, the proposed treatment, and the consequences of refusal? Then notify the physician, who must determine the appropriate process: if the patient has capacity (even with mild confusion), their refusal must be respected; if the patient lacks capacity, the substitute decision-making process (family, advance directive, legal guardian) applies. This is not a nursing decision alone — the physician and potentially the social work or ethics team must be involved.

incorrectRationales.A: Acting on a family-reported verbal refusal without assessing the patient's current capacity or involving the physician does not meet the standard of care for an ethically complex clinical situation. The nurse cannot unilaterally decide to withhold a potentially life-saving intervention based on a secondhand report.
incorrectRationales.B: Administering a transfusion over a patient's known or suspected objection — even with family consent — may violate the patient's autonomy rights if they retain decision-making capacity. Family consent cannot override the expressed wishes of a competent patient.
incorrectRationales.D: Ethics committee involvement may ultimately be appropriate if the capacity determination is unclear or the family and clinical team disagree — but it is not the first action. Clinical assessment, physician notification, and the standard consent/substitute decision process come first.

questions.stem: Select all clinical findings that are consistent with early sepsis in a 68-year-old patient admitted with a surgical wound infection.
choices: A. Heart rate of 104 bpm (baseline 72)
B. Blood pressure 108/66 mmHg (baseline 130/82)
C. Temperature 39.2°C
D. Respiratory rate 23 breaths per minute
E. Alert and oriented, conversational
F. Urinary output of 20 mL over the last two hours (weight 80 kg)
G. SpO2 96% on room air
questions.rationale: Early sepsis produces a cluster of physiological changes reflecting a dysregulated host response to infection: (A) new tachycardia (HR 104, up from 72 baseline) reflects compensatory sympathetic activation; (B) relative hypotension (BP 108/66, down from 130/82 baseline) represents vascular dilation and reduced systemic vascular resistance; (C) fever at 39.2°C indicates systemic inflammatory response; (D) tachypnoea at 23 reflects early respiratory compensation and metabolic acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion; (F) oliguria (20 mL in two hours for an 80 kg patient = 0.12 mL/kg/hr, well below the 0.5 mL/kg/hr threshold) indicates reduced renal perfusion — an early marker of organ dysfunction. This patient's qSOFA score would be 2-3, depending on mental status, indicating high risk for sepsis.

incorrectRationales.E: A patient who is alert and oriented and conversational has a GCS of 15 and an AVPU of 'Alert' — this is reassuring neurological finding, not a sign of early sepsis. However, it should be noted that sepsis can initially present without neurological changes.
incorrectRationales.G: An SpO2 of 96% on room air is within the acceptable range. While late sepsis can produce respiratory failure and hypoxaemia, an SpO2 of 96% does not represent a current indicator of early sepsis in this scenario — though trending should be monitored.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse has accepted an extra shift for the third consecutive week because they felt 'too guilty to say no.' The nurse is now working their fifth consecutive 12-hour shift and notices they are making minor documentation errors and forgetting to follow up on minor tasks. Which statement most accurately characterizes this situation?
choices: A. This is normal adjustment to the demands of nursing and will improve with more experience

B. The nurse's fatigue is affecting cognitive performance in ways that constitute patient safety risk, and the pattern of agreeing to extra shifts due to guilt represents a professional limit-setting deficit that must be addressed
C. The nurse should push through the fatigue, as patient care needs are more important than personal comfort
D. The nurse should take a medical leave of absence immediately
questions.rationale: Fatigue-related cognitive errors (documentation mistakes, missed follow-ups) represent measurable patient safety risk — research consistently shows that nursing error rates increase significantly after the 12th hour of a shift and across consecutive shifts without adequate recovery. The professional limit-setting issue — agreeing to shifts due to guilt rather than capacity — is a burnout precursor pattern that undermines long-term career sustainability. Both elements of this situation require active attention: the nurse should communicate to management that their current schedule is affecting clinical safety, and should build a personal policy that limits extra shifts to what their actual recovery allows — not what guilt permits.

incorrectRationales.A: Cognitive errors from fatigue are not an adjustment feature of nursing practice — they are a safety signal. Experience does not eliminate the physiological effects of sleep deprivation and cumulative fatigue. Calling these errors 'normal adjustment' normalizes dangerous practice patterns.
incorrectRationales.C: Pushing through fatigue while making cognitive errors that affect patient care is not professional commitment — it is unsafe practice. A fatigued nurse is a less safe nurse. Sustainability requires recovery, not endurance.
incorrectRationales.D: Immediate medical leave is not indicated for a nurse experiencing fatigue from voluntary schedule choices. The appropriate response is to address the schedule pattern, communicate with management, and build protective limits into future scheduling.

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse notices that a colleague has been showing progressively worsening signs over the past three weeks: calling in sick more frequently, making cynical comments about patients in the break room, appearing tearful after difficult shifts, and withdrawing from team social interactions. The nurse suspects the colleague is experiencing burnout or compassion fatigue. Which action most appropriately reflects professional peer support responsibility?
choices: A. Report the colleague to the charge nurse as a patient safety concern immediately
B. Approach the colleague privately with genuine concern, offer non-judgmental support, and encourage them to access available resources including the EAP
C. Avoid engaging with the colleague to respect their privacy and personal boundaries
D. Post information about burnout resources on the unit bulletin board without speaking directly to the colleague
questions.rationale: The professional standard for responding to a colleague showing signs of burnout or compassion fatigue is a direct, private, non-judgmental conversation that acknowledges the observable changes and offers genuine support. The colleague may not recognize the signs themselves, and the simple act of a peer saying 'I've noticed you seem to be having a hard time — I'm here if you want to talk' can interrupt isolation and normalize help-seeking. The nurse should also mention available institutional resources (EAP, peer support program) without pressuring. If the colleague's clinical performance is already affecting patient care safety, then charge nurse notification may also be appropriate — but peer support should not be bypassed in favour of administrative reporting without first attempting a supportive conversation.

incorrectRationales.A: Immediate administrative reporting without attempting peer support first is disproportionate when the described signs are emotional and behavioral rather than direct patient harm events. Peer support is the correct first-tier response for a colleague showing early burnout signs.
incorrectRationales.C: Avoiding the colleague to respect their privacy allows a deteriorating situation to continue unchallenged. Professional peer support is an expectation in nursing culture and regulatory standards — not an optional intrusion.
incorrectRationales.D: Anonymous resource posting is a passive response that is unlikely to reach a colleague who is withdrawing from team engagement. Direct peer-to-peer support is significantly more effective than impersonal information delivery.

questions.stem: During a code blue, the nurse's role is called as 'recorder.' Which activity accurately describes this role?
choices: A. Performing chest compressions at 100-120 per minute
B. Managing the defibrillator and calling for rhythm checks
C. Documenting every intervention, medication, rhythm finding, and time stamp in real time as directed by the team leader
D. Communicating with the patient's family in the hallway during the resuscitation
questions.rationale: The recorder in a code blue is responsible for creating a real-time, accurate, and complete record of all resuscitation events: each medication administered (drug, dose, time, route), rhythm findings and interventions (defibrillation, CPR cycles, airway management), personnel involvement, patient response, and the time of any return of spontaneous circulation or decision to terminate the resuscitation. This record is essential for clinical review, quality improvement, handoff to the receiving team, and legal documentation. The recorder must focus entirely on this documentation role — not multitask with other clinical functions.

incorrectRationales.A: Performing chest compressions is the compressor role — a physically demanding position that requires rotation every two minutes to maintain quality compressions. This is distinct from the recorder function.
incorrectRationales.B: Managing the defibrillator and calling rhythm checks is the role of the team leader or a designated defibrillator operator — typically a senior nurse or physician. This requires hands-on involvement with equipment that is incompatible with simultaneous documentation.
incorrectRationales.D: Communicating with the patient's family during a code is a dedicated role (often assigned to a social worker, chaplain, or designated nurse) that is separate from the recorder function. The recorder must remain focused on documentation and cannot effectively manage family communication simultaneously.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a new stroke diagnosis. At 14:30, the patient's level of consciousness changes — they were alert and answering questions at 13:45 and are now responding only to voice with confused speech. The nurse immediately checks vital signs: BP 172/108 (previously 128/78), HR 88, SpO2 97%. What is the most critical next action?
choices: A. Administer a PRN antihypertensive to address the elevated blood pressure
B. Immediately activate the stroke alert/rapid response, notify the physician using SBAR, and document the time of symptom change — the door-to-needle window for thrombolytics may be active
C. Document the findings and reassess in 30 minutes to determine if the LOC change is transient
D. Apply oxygen at 2L/min and reassess the blood pressure in 15 minutes
questions.rationale: A patient with a known stroke diagnosis who develops an acute change in level of consciousness and new hypertension may be experiencing a stroke progression or extension — a neurological emergency. The window for thrombolytic therapy (tPA) is 4.5 hours from symptom onset — and the time of LOC change (14:30) establishes a potentially active treatment window. The nurse must document the time of symptom change precisely, immediately activate the stroke response/rapid response team, and notify the physician urgently. Every minute of delayed reperfusion in an ischaemic stroke results in approximately 1.9 million neurons lost. This is not a 'wait and reassess' situation.

incorrectRationales.A: Administering an antihypertensive in a patient with acute stroke without a specific order is dangerous. Hypertension in acute ischaemic stroke is often a protective response (Cushing reflex) that maintains perfusion to the ischaemic penumbra. Lowering blood pressure without medical guidance can extend the infarct. Blood pressure management in stroke requires specific physician orders and specialist input.
incorrectRationales.C: Waiting 30 minutes to reassess a neurological change in a patient with a stroke diagnosis is a potentially catastrophic delay. Time is brain — the treatment window for thrombolytics closes, and every 30 minutes of delay reduces the probability of a good neurological outcome.
incorrectRationales.D: Applying 2L oxygen and reassessing blood pressure does not address the clinical urgency of an acute neurological change in a stroke patient. Supplemental oxygen may be appropriate if SpO2 is below 94%, but with an SpO2 of 97%, this is not the most critical action — stroke team activation is.

questions.stem: A nurse has just completed the first hour of orientation to a new unit. While reviewing a patient's MAR, they notice an order for potassium chloride 40 mEq IV push over five minutes. The nurse is unfamiliar with this route and rate for potassium administration. What should the nurse do?
choices: A. Administer the medication as ordered, as the physician who wrote the order has more clinical expertise
B. Do not administer the medication; clarify the order with the prescribing physician and check with the pharmacist, as IV potassium chloride push is a high-alert route associated with cardiac arrest
C. Administer the medication more slowly at 20 mEq over 20 minutes to add a safety buffer
D. Delegate administration to a more experienced nurse on the unit
questions.rationale: Concentrated IV potassium chloride administered as a rapid push is one of the most recognized high-alert medication sentinel events in healthcare — it causes cardiac arrest by producing hyperkalemia-induced arrhythmia within seconds to minutes. The Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) has classified concentrated potassium chloride as a 'never event' for IV push administration and has recommended that it not be available as a floor stock medication in undiluted form. An order for 40 mEq IV push over five minutes is a medication error — the safe route is diluted infusion (typically 10-20 mEq/hour in a compatible IV fluid). The nurse's correct action is to withhold the medication and seek urgent clarification.

incorrectRationales.A: Physician expertise does not make an unsafe order safe. The nurse is the final safety checkpoint before a medication reaches the patient — accepting a known high-risk order without questioning it constitutes a patient safety failure regardless of who wrote the order.
incorrectRationales.C: Independently modifying the administration rate without a new physician order still places potassium in an undiluted, direct IV push context. Even a slower push of undiluted concentrated potassium chloride is unsafe — the issue is the concentration and route, not only the rate.
incorrectRationales.D: Delegating administration of a medication you believe may be unsafe to another nurse does not resolve the safety concern — it transfers the risk. If the order is potentially harmful, it must be clarified before administration, regardless of who administers it.

questions.stem: A nurse receives morning report for five patients. Within the first 30 minutes of the shift, which task should take absolute priority before any medications are administered or documentation is entered?
choices: A. Update the nursing care plans for all five patients
B. Perform a visual safety check on each patient — confirming they are present, breathing, and not in distress
C. Reconcile each patient's medication administration record with the original physician orders
D. Review the overnight laboratory results for all patients before entering any rooms
questions.rationale: The first 30 minutes of a shift is the highest-risk window for discovering clinical events that developed at the end of the previous shift or during handoff. Before any administrative task — documentation, medication reconciliation, or lab review — the nurse must confirm that each patient is physiologically safe. A rapid visual check establishes: (1) that every patient is present (bed not empty, patient not on floor), (2) that they are breathing and not in respiratory distress, and (3) that no patient presents with an obvious acute clinical event requiring immediate attention. This sweep takes approximately five minutes for five patients and cannot be deferred. Documentation and medication administration can be systematically organized once safety is confirmed.

incorrectRationales.A: Care plan updates are a documentation task — important, but not time-sensitive in the first 30 minutes when patients have not yet been seen. Care plan updates belong in the mid-shift administrative block after initial patient safety is confirmed.
incorrectRationales.C: Medication reconciliation is an important safety activity, but it is not more urgent than confirming that each patient is clinically safe. A patient in distress discovered during reconciliation could have been found and helped 20 minutes earlier with a visual sweep.
incorrectRationales.D: Lab review is important and should be completed early in the shift — but after the visual safety check, not before it. A nurse reviewing labs at the workstation cannot detect a patient who has deteriorated in their room.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients on a medical-surgical unit with medications due at 09:00, 10:00, 11:00, and 12:00. By 11:15, the nurse has completed two assessments and all 09:00 medications but has not yet started the 10:00 or 11:00 medications. An unexpected family meeting is requested for 11:30. Which action best reflects effective shift organization?
choices: A. Attend the family meeting and administer all delayed medications immediately afterward

B. Communicate to the charge nurse that you have time-sensitive medications to administer, ask whether the family meeting can be rescheduled or covered by a social worker, and complete the critical medications first
C. Skip the 10:00 medications to attend the family meeting on time
D. Administer all overdue medications rapidly without the five-rights verification to catch up before the meeting
questions.rationale: A nurse who is behind on time-sensitive medications faces a competing priorities conflict that requires charge nurse communication, not independent resolution. The correct approach is to communicate the specific clinical constraint (two medication windows missed, another due now) and request either: coverage for the family meeting by a social worker or designate, or schedule the meeting for later in the shift when the medication backlog has been cleared. Most institutions have social workers, discharge planners, or charge nurses available to participate in family meetings. A nurse should not skip medications, rush through verification, or silently fall further behind without communicating the constraint.

incorrectRationales.A: Attending the 30-minute family meeting and then administering all overdue medications creates a scenario where some medications are two to three hours late. Some medications (antibiotics, anticoagulants, cardiac drugs) cannot safely be delayed this long without physician notification.
incorrectRationales.C: Unilaterally skipping the 10:00 medications without notification to complete a family meeting prioritizes a communication task over medication safety. Medication windows are clinical safety standards — deviation requires charge nurse communication.
incorrectRationales.D: Rushing through medication administration without complete five-rights verification is a known medication error pattern. Speed is never justification for bypassing high-alert medication safety checks.

questions.stem: Select all elements that should be included in a nurse's personal 'brain sheet' or shift-organization tool used on a medical-surgical unit.
choices: A. Patient name and room number
B. Active diagnosis and admitting reason
C. Scheduled medications with timing windows
D. The previous nurse's personal opinion of the patient
E. Pending investigations (labs, imaging) and expected results
F. Isolation precautions and known allergies
G. Patient preferences for television programming
questions.rationale: A nursing brain sheet is a personalized clinical reference tool that consolidates the critical patient data a nurse needs for safe, organized shift management. Essential elements include: (A) patient identification (room and name) to prevent administration errors; (B) active diagnosis and admission reason to orient clinical thinking; (C) medication timing to prevent missed or late doses; (E) pending investigations so the nurse can track expected results and act promptly when they return; and (F) isolation precautions and allergies as immediate clinical safety references. The brain sheet is a dynamic clinical tool — it should reflect what the nurse needs to give safe care, not personal commentary or non-clinical information.

incorrectRationales.D: A previous nurse's personal opinion about a patient is subjective, potentially biased, and irrelevant to clinical care. Brain sheets are clinical reference tools — not spaces for interpersonal commentary that can create unjustified negative perceptions of patients.
incorrectRationales.G: Television programming preferences are a patient comfort preference that does not belong on a clinical organization tool. Comfort preferences can be noted in the care plan but are not part of a nursing brain sheet designed to organize shift-critical clinical data.

questions.stem: A nurse is assigned to a unit with four patients and is at the 10-hour mark of a 12-hour shift. Documentation is complete, all scheduled medications have been given, and all patients are currently stable. An experienced colleague asks if the nurse would like to take a meal break. The nurse has not yet eaten and has not left the unit. Which response most accurately reflects evidence-based nursing practice regarding breaks?
choices: A. Decline the break, as leaving the unit during a shift is unprofessional
B. Take the break — cognitive performance declines significantly after 8 to 10 hours without rest, and recovery periods directly support patient safety
C. Accept the break but return after five minutes, as longer breaks create unfair workload for colleagues
D. Eat at the nursing station while continuing to monitor patients, as this is an acceptable substitute for a formal break
questions.rationale: Research consistently shows that cognitive performance declines measurably after 8 to 10 hours without rest — with reductions in attention, working memory, and error detection that directly affect patient safety. Meal breaks are not optional professional courtesies — they are an occupational health requirement recognized by most nursing associations, labour standards legislation, and hospital policies. At a 10-hour mark with patients stable and documentation complete, this is the appropriate moment to take a full break. Skipping meals on 12-hour shifts is a normalized but harmful practice associated with higher rates of medication errors, reduced clinical judgment, and accelerated compassion fatigue.

incorrectRationales.A: Declining a meal break because leaving the unit feels 'unprofessional' is a normalization of an unhealthy practice. Meal breaks are a professional entitlement and a patient safety measure — the narrative that nurses should not leave the unit is a toxic cultural artifact, not a professional standard.
incorrectRationales.C: A regulated meal break (typically 30 minutes under most provincial and state labour standards) is not a burden to colleagues — it is a standard staffing assumption in shift scheduling. Cutting the break short to avoid imposing on colleagues perpetuates a culture of self-sacrifice that contributes to burnout.
incorrectRationales.D: Eating at the nursing station while monitoring patients does not provide the cognitive and physiological recovery that a genuine break delivers. A break requires physical separation from the clinical environment — even briefly — to allow the stress response to downregulate.
